proposals.title: A Composite Psychophysiological Load Index for Predicting Emotion Regulation Failure: An Analogy from Materials Science
proposals.motivation: Current psychophysiological research on emotion regulation (ER) relies on isolated measures (e.g., HRV, corrugator EMG), which may not capture the cumulative, multi-system load on regulatory capacity. This limits our ability to objectively predict when an individual is approaching dysregulation, a critical gap for clinical and real-time intervention applications. A unified, predictive metric of ER load, analogous to composite stress indices in engineering, is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite index integrating real-time, multi-modal psychophysiological data (HRV, EDA, facial EMG, RSA) will predict self-reported difficulty in emotion regulation and subsequent behavioral markers of regulatory failure more accurately than any single measure. Furthermore, the predictive power of this index will be moderated by an individual's emotion regulation goals.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Tsai-Wu failure criterion framework to psychophysiology. Participants will complete a lab-based mixed-emotion induction task while we collect continuous ECG, EDA, and corrugator/supercilii EMG. We will derive standardized scores for each channel (e.g., HRV RMSSD, EDA response magnitude, corrugator activity). An initial algorithm will compute a composite load score as a weighted quadratic function of these standardized inputs, with weights empirically derived via regression against moment-to-moment self-reports of regulatory difficulty. We will validate this index against objective outcomes like persistence on a subsequent frustrating task.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, objective, and predictive biomarker of emotion regulation load, moving the field beyond descriptive, single-system accounts. It will offer a concrete methodological advance for ecological momentary assessment (EMA) and intervention studies by defining a quantifiable threshold for regulatory strain. The resulting index could serve as a foundational tool for just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) in mental health.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual translation of a materials science failure model to psychophysiology, proposing a formal mathematical integration of disparate signals into a single predictive metric. It moves beyond simply correlating multiple measures by algorithmically combining them to model the *system-level* load on the regulatory apparatus.

proposals.title: Emotion Regulation Goals as a Mechanism Linking Masculine Norm Conformity and Internalizing Symptoms: A Genetically-Informed Longitudinal Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that conformity to masculine norms (e.g., restrictive emotionality) is linked to poorer mental health, but the causal direction and mechanisms are unclear. This is a critical public health issue, as these norms may undermine adaptive emotion regulation. A key gap is whether internalizing symptoms drive norm conformity as a coping strategy, or if conformity causally exacerbates symptoms by shaping maladaptive emotion regulation goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize a reciprocal, reinforcing cycle where increased conformity to restrictive emotionality norms predicts subsequent increases in internalizing symptoms, mediated by a shift toward emotion suppression goals, and where prior internalizing symptoms also predict increased conformity.
proposals.method: We will recruit 400 male-identified twin and sibling pairs (ages 18-30) for a three-wave longitudinal study. At each wave, participants will complete the ERQ-S, measures of masculine norm conformity and internalizing symptoms, and a behavioral task assessing expressive suppression (corrugator EMG) during emotion elicitation with explicit emotion goals. We will use random-intercept cross-lagged panel models (RI-CLPM) to test within-person temporal precedence. The discordant twin/sibling design will then be applied to these within-person changes to control for shared genetic and environmental confounds.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first genetically-informed, longitudinal test of bidirectional causation between masculine norms and internalizing symptoms. It will directly integrate Gross's process model of emotion regulation by identifying specific emotion regulation goals as a proximal mechanism. Findings will inform more precise interventions targeting emotion goals within normative social contexts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a discordant sibling design to within-person change models, isolating causal effects from shared background factors, and explicitly testing emotion regulation goals—a core construct from Gross's framework—as the mechanistic link in this reciprocal relationship.

proposals.title: Emotional Vulnerability as a Precursor to Normative Masculinity: A Longitudinal and Psychophysiological Test of a Reversed Causal Pathway
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on masculinity and mental health predominantly examines how conformity to masculine norms (e.g., emotional restriction, self-reliance) predicts internalizing symptoms and emotion regulation (ER) difficulties. This unidirectional model may be incomplete. Recent work in affective science suggests that pre-existing emotional vulnerabilities could shape the adoption of specific regulatory strategies and social scripts, including gender norms, as a form of coping or self-protection. Testing this reversed pathway is crucial for developing more nuanced, etiological models of gender-linked psychopathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher baseline levels of internalizing symptoms (depression, anxiety) and specific ER difficulties (e.g., lack of emotional clarity, non-acceptance of emotions) will predict increased subsequent conformity to specific masculine norms related to emotional restriction and self-reliance, over and above the reverse pathway.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method, longitudinal design. A community sample of adult men will complete baseline assessments of internalizing symptoms (DASS-21), ER difficulties (DERS), and emotion goals. They will then report on their conformity to masculine norms (CMNI) at three follow-ups over 12 months. Concurrently, a lab session will measure expressive and physiological responses (e.g., corrugator EMG, EDA) to standardized negative and positive emotion-eliciting films, assessing spontaneous regulation linked to masculine ideals. Cross-lagged panel modeling (CLPM) will test the bidirectional relationships between baseline emotional vulnerability indices and longitudinal changes in norm conformity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct longitudinal test of emotional vulnerability as a predictor of masculine norm conformity. It will extend Gross's work on emotion goals and regulation by situating regulatory patterns within a developmental framework of gender socialization. Findings could inform more effective, person-centered interventions by identifying men for whom normative conformity is a maladaptive coping response.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the dominant causal model in masculinity research. It integrates longitudinal survey methods with Gross's signature psychophysiological assessment (e.g., corrugator) to move beyond self-report and capture real-time emotional processes that may underlie the adoption of gendered social norms.

proposals.title: The Norm-Affect Loop: A Genetically-Informed, Longitudinal Test of Bidirectional and Non-Linear Dynamics Between Social Norms, Alexithymia, and Emotion Regulation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows perceived social norms powerfully shape emotional behavior, and alexithymia (difficulty identifying feelings) is a key transdiagnostic risk factor for poor emotion regulation (ER). However, studies are largely cross-sectional, obscuring whether norms drive alexithymia/ER, or vice-versa, and whether these relationships are linear or involve threshold effects. Understanding these dynamic, causal pathways is crucial for designing interventions that target the most malleable points in this system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize bidirectional, non-linear causal relationships: increases in perceived norms for emotional suppression will predict subsequent increases in alexithymia, which in turn will predict greater use of maladaptive ER strategies like expressive suppression; conversely, high baseline alexithymia will predict increased perception of restrictive emotional norms over time.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-wave longitudinal design with 600 participants (200 monozygotic twin pairs, 100 dizygotic pairs) assessed 6 times over two years. Measures include self-report (ERQ-S, Toronto Alexithymia Scale, perceived emotion norms), behavioral tasks, and psychophysiology (corrugator EMG as an objective index of negative emotion expression/regulation). We will use a combination of Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Models (RI-CLPM) to separate within-person dynamics from stable traits, and Latent Change Score (LCS) modeling within a structural equation framework to test for non-linear (e.g., quadratic) growth and coupling parameters, controlling for shared genetic and environmental confounds via the twin design.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first genetically-informed, longitudinal map of the dynamic architecture linking social norms, alexithymia, and ER. It will move the field beyond static correlation by identifying the temporal precedence and potential tipping points in these relationships, offering a stronger basis for causal inference and informing the timing and targets of ecological momentary interventions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating a genetically sensitive, longitudinal design with advanced dynamic modeling (LCS) to explicitly test for *bidirectional* and *non-linear* causal pathways—addressing critical gaps in the largely correlational and linear literature on social norms and emotion regulation.

proposals.title: A Complex Systems Approach to Emotional Functioning: Modeling the Dynamic Interplay of Social Norms, Alexithymia, and Emotion Regulation
proposals.motivation: Current models of emotion regulation (ER) often treat constructs like alexithymia and social norms as static, linear predictors of outcomes. This fails to capture their dynamic, reciprocal nature, limiting our understanding of why interventions show variable efficacy. Drawing an analogy from ecology, where complex, non-linear interactions are the norm, this research proposes to model emotional functioning as a complex adaptive system, a novel approach in affective science pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationships between perceived social norms for emotional expression, alexithymia, and emotion regulation strategy use are characterized by non-linear, moderated mediation, and that small shifts in normative contexts can produce disproportionate, systemic changes in emotional processing outcomes through feedback loops.
proposals.method: Study 1 will use a multi-method, longitudinal design with Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) and lab-based psychophysiology (e.g., corrugator EMG, heart rate). Data will be analyzed using Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with latent moderated mediation to test initial pathways. Study 2 will build an agent-based computational model, parameterized with Study 1 data, to simulate the dynamic system. This model will use differential equations to represent feedback between system components, allowing for scenario testing (e.g., manipulating normative 'tipping points').
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable framework for understanding emotional functioning as a dynamic system, moving beyond linear correlation. It will identify specific leverage points (e.g., normative shifts) for potential intervention and demonstrate the utility of computational modeling for theory-building in emotion research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a complex systems analogy and agent-based modeling to the study of alexithymia and emotion regulation, explicitly testing for non-linear dynamics and feedback effects that existing sequential models cannot capture.

proposals.title: Mapping the Momentary Dynamics of Alexithymia and Emotion Regulation: An Ecological Momentary Assessment and Dynamic Network Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: Alexithymia, a difficulty in identifying and describing emotions, is a core transdiagnostic risk factor for psychopathology and is thought to disrupt adaptive emotion regulation (ER). However, research has relied on static, retrospective self-reports, failing to capture how alexithymic difficulties and ER attempts unfold and influence each other in real-time within naturalistic social contexts. This gap limits our understanding of the dynamic, process-oriented architecture of emotional dysfunction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that momentary increases in perceived unsupportive social norms will dynamically strengthen the network connection between alexithymic difficulties (e.g., difficulty identifying feelings) and maladaptive regulatory attempts (e.g., expressive suppression), which in turn will predict increased negative affect and reduced positive affect at subsequent timepoints.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 community adults with a range of alexithymia scores (TAS-20). Participants will complete a 14-day EMA protocol, prompted 6 times daily to report current emotion, emotion goals, regulation attempts, perceived social norms regarding emotion expression, and state alexithymic difficulties. We will also collect ambulatory corrugator supercilii EMG as a physiological index of negative affect. Data will be analyzed using multilevel vector autoregression (mlVAR) to construct contemporaneous and temporal networks, and time-lagged mixed-effects models to test specific directional pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, dynamic network map of how alexithymia operates in daily life, identifying key momentary triggers and consequences. It will elucidate specific, modifiable points in the emotional process (e.g., social context interpretations) that could be targeted in just-in-time adaptive interventions to improve emotional awareness and regulation.
proposals.novelty: This proposal integrates Gross's expertise in ER and EMA with advanced dynamic network modeling to move beyond static correlations, directly testing how alexithymia functions as a dynamic system influenced by and influencing momentary context, a novel application of these methods to this construct.

proposals.title: The Social-Emotional Loop: A Dynamic Network Analysis of Norms, Alexithymia, Emotion Regulation, and Symptoms Over Time
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established links between alexithymia, poor emotion regulation, and psychological symptoms, often treating these as stable, unidirectional risk factors. Furthermore, the role of perceived social norms in shaping these intrapsychic processes is understudied. A critical gap is understanding the dynamic, reciprocal influences among these constructs at the within-person level, which is essential for designing effective, personalized interventions that target the most potent temporal pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that perceived social norms about emotional expression, alexithymia, emotion regulation strategy use (e.g., reappraisal, suppression), and psychological symptoms will exhibit significant reciprocal cross-lagged influences over time, forming a self-reinforcing feedback loop that maintains distress.
proposals.method: We will employ a 5-wave longitudinal panel design with 3-month intervals, recruiting 400 community adults with varying symptom levels. Core constructs will be measured via self-report (e.g., ERQ-S, TAS-20, norms scales, DASS-21). The primary analysis will use Random-Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Modeling (RI-CLPM) in Mplus or R's lavaan package. This model explicitly partitions within-person variance from stable between-person differences, allowing us to test for bidirectional temporal influences (cross-lagged paths) among all four constructs simultaneously, while controlling for autoregressive effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first dynamic, within-person network model of how social norms and alexithymia interact with emotion regulation to predict symptom trajectories. It will move the field beyond static mediation models by identifying which specific temporal pathways (e.g., norms -> alexithymia -> symptoms vs. symptoms -> regulation -> norms) are most influential, offering precise targets for ecological momentary interventions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the RI-CLPM framework to model all four constructs as a dynamic, reciprocally influencing system, explicitly testing for feedback loops rather than assuming a linear causal chain. This integrates the social context of norms directly into a process model of emotion dysregulation.

proposals.title: Targeting Normative Beliefs for Emotional Health: A Norm-Based Intervention to Reduce Conformity to Emotional Control, Self-Reliance, and Work Primacy
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in emotion regulation identifies maladaptive social norms—such as emotional control, rigid self-reliance, and the primacy of work—as key barriers to adaptive emotional functioning. These norms are linked to increased psychological distress, reduced social support seeking, and poorer well-being, yet interventions have largely focused on individual skills rather than the normative context that shapes them. This project addresses this gap by developing a direct, scalable intervention to modify these specific, harmful norms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a brief, targeted psychoeducational and values-clarification intervention, delivered via Ecological Momentary Intervention (EMI), will reduce participants' conformity to norms of emotional control, self-reliance, and work primacy, leading to improved emotional functioning (e.g., greater emotional acceptance, increased support-seeking, reduced corrugator activity in response to stressors).
proposals.method: Using a pre-post randomized controlled trial, we will recruit 150 adults scoring high on conformity to the target norms. The intervention group will receive a multi-component EMI over 14 days, including: (1) daily psychoeducational messages challenging the utility of the norms, (2) values-clarification exercises prompting reflection on personal vs. normative goals, and (3) behavioral 'experiments' to practice norm-violating actions (e.g., sharing a vulnerability). We will assess outcomes using the Emotion Regulation Questionnaire-Short Form (ERQ-S), daily Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) of emotional experiences and norm adherence, and psychophysiological measures (corrugator supercilii EMG) during a standardized lab stressor pre- and post-intervention.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical test of a norm-focused intervention for emotional health, extending Gross's work on emotion regulation into the social-determinants domain. It will yield a manualized, scalable EMI protocol and mechanistic insights into how shifting normative beliefs alters experiential, expressive, and physiological emotion dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined norms as correlates of emotion regulation, this proposal innovates by directly targeting and attempting to modify these specific norms as the primary mechanism of change, integrating normative intervention techniques from social psychology with the ecological, multi-method assessment framework central to Gross's recent research.

proposals.title: Context-Aware Ecological Momentary Assessment for Alexithymia: Using Smartphone Sensor-Triggered Probes to Capture Emotion Awareness Deficits In Vivo
proposals.motivation: Alexithymia, a deficit in identifying and describing feelings, is a transdiagnostic risk factor for psychopathology and impairs emotion regulation. Traditional self-report measures are retrospective and lack ecological validity, failing to capture the real-time, context-specific nature of emotional awareness challenges. Pre-2026 EMA studies on emotion regulation, including Dr. Gross's work, have relied on random or fixed-interval sampling, which may miss the precise moments when alexithymic difficulties are most acute.
proposals.hypothesis: Algorithmically triggering EMA prompts based on smartphone sensor data (e.g., detecting social interactions or location changes) will yield more ecologically valid and sensitive measures of alexithymia-related deficits compared to random sampling, and these contextually salient moments will be associated with greater self-reported difficulty in identifying feelings and maladaptive regulation attempts.
proposals.method: We will adapt HCI/Ubiquitous Computing methods by developing a smartphone app that uses the AWARE framework to collect sensor data (GPS, accelerometer, call/SMS logs, app usage). A rule-based algorithm will trigger EMA prompts upon detecting predefined contextually salient events (e.g., entering a novel location, a period of high social communication, or a transition from high to low physical activity). Each prompt will include adapted items from the Toronto Alexithymia Scale-20 (TAS-20) and the ERQ-S, focusing on current emotional experience and regulation attempts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, context-sensitive assessment tool for alexithymia that moves the field beyond static questionnaires. It will generate new data on the daily situational triggers of alexithymic experiences and their immediate links to emotion regulation strategy use, directly extending Dr. Gross's research on emotion goals and regulation in daily life.
proposals.novelty: While EMA is established, the use of smartphone sensor data to algorithmically trigger emotion probes for a clinical construct like alexithymia is novel. This method adapts HCI's 'in-situ probing' for system evaluation to a clinical psychology assessment goal, creating an ecologically triggered, rather than random, sampling protocol.

proposals.title: Psychophysiological Signatures of Emotion Regulation Difficulty: Integrating Heart Rate Variability and Corrugator EMG to Predict Subjective Regulatory Effort
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on emotion regulation (ER) often relies on self-report, which can be biased and fails to capture the dynamic, effortful nature of regulation in real time. While psychophysiological measures like corrugator EMG are established indices of emotional response, they are rarely integrated with autonomic indices of regulatory effort, such as heart rate variability (HRV), to create a multi-system, objective assessment of ER difficulty. This gap limits our ability to identify individuals who struggle with regulation before negative outcomes occur.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific psychophysiological profile—characterized by high corrugator reactivity (indicating negative emotion) coupled with low high-frequency HRV (indicating poor parasympathetic engagement and regulatory effort)—will predict greater self-reported difficulty in down-regulating negative emotion during a laboratory task.
proposals.method: Participants will complete a well-validated ER task (e.g., the emotion regulation task from Gross's prior work) where they are instructed to use reappraisal to down-regulate their emotional response to negative images. We will collect continuous ECG to derive respiratory sinus arrhythmia (RSA) as an index of HRV, and corrugator supercilii EMG. Using established pre-2026 signal processing (e.g., Kubios HRV software for RSA, rectification and smoothing for EMG), we will extract epoch-level features. A hierarchical linear model will test if the interaction between corrugator activity and RSA during regulation trials predicts trial-by-trial subjective difficulty ratings.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, multi-channel psychophysiological model for objectively quantifying the moment-to-moment difficulty of implementing ER strategies. It will advance the field beyond single-measure studies by linking expressive (corrugator) and autonomic (HRV) systems. The findings could inform the development of biofeedback interventions and offer a more precise assessment tool for clinical samples where self-report is unreliable.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined corrugator or HRV in isolation during ER, this proposal's novelty lies in explicitly modeling their interaction as a signature of regulatory difficulty, moving from measuring emotional response to quantifying the regulatory process itself. This integrated approach is non-obvious given that HRV is often studied as a trait, not a dynamic state marker of effort during specific regulation trials.

proposals.title: Networked Physiology: A Multi-Method Test of How Social Norms Drive Internalizing Symptoms via Emotion Dysregulation
proposals.motivation: Network models of psychopathology have advanced our understanding of symptom interactions, but they remain largely statistical, lacking grounding in biological mechanisms. Concurrently, psychophysiological research by Gross and others has established objective indices of emotion dysregulation (e.g., low heart rate variability, heightened electrodermal activity). Integrating these fields is critical to move from descriptive network maps to causal, biologically plausible models of how socio-emotional processes culminate in disorders like depression and anxiety.
proposals.hypothesis: The statistical connections within a psychopathology network linking perceived social norms (e.g., perfectionism, restrictive emotionality) to internalizing symptoms (e.g., rumination, anhedonia) will be significantly stronger for individuals showing concurrent psychophysiological markers of emotion dysregulation, indicating these pathways are biologically grounded rather than purely cognitive.
proposals.method: We will recruit 200 participants with elevated internalizing symptoms. Using Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA), we will measure perceived social norms, specific emotions (shame, anxiety), and symptoms. In a lab session, participants will complete an emotion regulation task (e.g., reappraisal of negative stimuli) while we record corrugator supercilii EMG, heart rate variability (HRV), and electrodermal activity (EDA). We will construct a multi-level network using the R package `mgm` (Mixed Graphical Models) to estimate connections between norms, emotions, and symptoms. We will then use moderated network analysis to test if individual differences in physiological regulation (e.g., HRV reactivity) moderate the strength of specific norm-to-emotion and emotion-to-symptom edges.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated network model that statistically links social-cognitive constructs with objective psychophysiology, offering a more mechanistic account of internalizing disorders. It will generate testable hypotheses about which symptom pathways are most tied to biological dysregulation, potentially informing more targeted interventions (e.g., biofeedback for specific regulatory deficits).
proposals.novelty: While network psychometrics and psychophysiology have advanced in parallel, this study is novel in directly using physiological data to validate and weight the edges of a psychological symptom network, moving beyond correlation to a multi-method, mechanistic test.

proposals.title: Micro-interactional Emotion Norm Enforcement in Dyadic Conversations: A Fine-Grained Sequential Analysis of Pathways to Internalizing and Externalizing Affect
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on emotion regulation has established its link to psychopathology, but often treats regulation as an individual, static trait. The dynamic, moment-to-process by which social interactions shape regulatory pathways remains poorly understood. This gap is critical, as therapy and close relationships are central contexts for emotion socialization, and micro-moments within them may simultaneously seed divergent clinical outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conversational moments where one partner enforces a specific emotional norm (e.g., minimizing negative affect) will predict immediate, divergent shifts in the other partner's emotional mechanisms: specifically, increased corrugator supercilii activity and self-reported shame (internalizing pathway) or suppressed expressive anger followed by a subsequent outburst (externalizing pathway).
proposals.method: We will recruit romantic dyads or client-therapist pairs for emotionally salient conversations. Using the Electronically Activated Recorder (EAR) and video, we will code micro-interactions (e.g., corrective pauses, specific word choices) for norm enforcement. We will apply fine-grained sequential analysis, specifically lag-sequential analysis and multilevel survival analysis, to test if norm enforcement predicts subsequent physiological (corrugator EMG), experiential (momentary self-report of shame/anger), and expressive (facial coding of anger) responses within the same interaction.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first process-oriented, within-interaction model linking specific social moments to divergent emotion regulation pathways. It will move the field beyond correlational designs by identifying real-time mechanisms. The findings will directly inform the design of ecological momentary interventions (EMIs) that target micro-interactional processes in therapy and daily life.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying micro-analytic, sequential methods from social interaction research to the study of clinical emotion regulation pathways, testing how a single social event can bifurcate into both internalizing and externalizing trajectories simultaneously.

proposals.title: From Norms to Actions: Distinct Emotional Pathways Linking Hypercompetitive Peer Norms to Reactive Aggression and Rule-Breaking
proposals.motivation: While social norms are known to influence externalizing behaviors, the specific emotional mechanisms remain poorly specified. Pre-2026 research often treats 'negative emotion' as a monolithic mediator, obscuring whether norms like hypercompetitiveness lead to aggression via heightened frustration/anger or via diminished empathy/callousness. Disentangling these pathways is crucial for developing precise, mechanism-targeted interventions for adolescent conduct problems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hypercompetitive peer norms will predict reactive aggression primarily through a pathway of increased frustration and anger, but will predict non-aggressive rule-breaking primarily through a pathway of increased emotional callousness and reduced empathy.
proposals.method: We will recruit 300 adolescents (ages 14-16) for a 6-month longitudinal study. At baseline, we will assess perceived hypercompetitive peer norms via self- and peer-report. Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) will be used 3 times daily for 14 days at baseline and 6 months to measure in-situ emotional states (frustration, anger, callous affect). Externalizing outcomes (reactive aggression, rule-breaking) will be measured via self-, peer-, and teacher-report at 6 months. We will use structural equation modeling (SEM) with latent variables and path analysis to test the distinctiveness and predictive power of the proposed emotional pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a granular, mechanistic model linking a specific social norm to specific behavioral outcomes via separable emotional pathways. It will advance emotion regulation theory by demonstrating how social contexts shape discrete emotional tendencies that drive behavior. The findings will inform the development of more targeted interventions, such as frustration tolerance training versus empathy promotion, based on the identified pathway.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond demonstrating 'different mechanisms' to statistically isolating and validating distinct emotional pathways (frustration/anger vs. callousness) from a single norm to different externalizing outcomes, using a multi-method, longitudinal design that integrates Gross's expertise in emotion assessment with advanced modeling.

proposals.title: Longitudinal fMRI of Pain and Emotion Regulation: Testing a Dual-System Model for Divergent Pathways to Internalizing and Externalizing Psychopathology
proposals.motivation: Adolescence is a critical period for the development of both internalizing (e.g., anxiety) and externalizing (e.g., aggression) disorders, which are major public health burdens. Pre-2026 research suggests overlapping but distinct neural circuits for pain processing and emotion regulation are implicated, yet longitudinal studies tracking their concurrent maturation to predict specific behavioral outcomes are lacking. Understanding whether early dysregulation in these systems acts as a mechanistic precursor is crucial for targeted intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adverse social environments will lead to divergent developmental trajectories: a primary deficit in pain modulation circuitry (e.g., anterior cingulate, insula) will predict internalizing symptoms, while a primary deficit in emotion regulation circuitry (e.g., prefrontal-amygdala connectivity), particularly for anger/irritability, will predict externalizing symptoms.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 3-wave longitudinal study (ages 14, 17, 20) with N=150 adolescents. At each wave, participants will complete: 1) fMRI during a well-validated pain modulation task (e.g., conditioned pain modulation) and an emotion regulation task (e.g., cognitive reappraisal of anger-inducing stimuli), 2) behavioral assessments of emotion regulation (ERQ-S) and emotion goals, and 3) measures of internalizing/externalizing symptoms and social adversity. We will use mixed-effects models and latent growth curve modeling to analyze longitudinal changes in neural circuit function (e.g., amygdala-PFC connectivity during regulation) and their predictive power for symptom trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, multimodal neuroimaging test of a unified pain-emotion dysregulation model, moving beyond cross-sectional correlation. It will identify specific neural circuit imbalances that precede and potentially mechanistically contribute to distinct psychopathology pathways. The findings could inform the development of more precise, circuit-targeted interventions for at-risk youth.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely tracks the *concurrent* development of two putatively related neural systems (pain and emotion regulation) to test their *differential* predictive utility for internalizing vs. externalizing outcomes, a critical step beyond studying these systems in isolation or as a single construct.

proposals.title: Emotion Regulation Goals and Norms as Predictors of Externalizing Symptoms: A Multi-Method Investigation of Expressive and Experiential Pathways
proposals.motivation: Prior research on emotion regulation norms has predominantly focused on internalizing outcomes like depression and anxiety. However, the same societal or interpersonal norms that discourage emotional expression (e.g., 'boys don't cry') may also predict externalizing problems like aggression, but through distinct emotional mechanisms. This gap is critical to address for a complete model of how emotion regulation contributes to psychopathology, particularly in developmental and clinical contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strong endorsement of emotion suppression norms will predict increased externalizing symptoms, and that this relationship will be serially mediated by (a) the adoption of emotion suppression goals and (b) subsequent increases in negative emotional experience and expressive tension, rather than by dampened experience alone.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 adult participants to complete the ERQ-S and measures of perceived emotion norms and externalizing symptoms. In a lab session, participants will view standardized negative emotion-eliciting film clips under conditions designed to prime either suppression or acceptance goals. We will measure experiential responses via self-report, expressive behavior via corrugator supercilii EMG activity (a validated index of negative emotional expression), and physiological arousal via heart rate. A serial mediation analysis will test the proposed pathway from norms to goals to corrugator activity (expressive tension) to self-reported anger/irritability, and finally to trait aggression scores.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first multi-method evidence linking emotion regulation norms and goals to externalizing symptomatology through an expressive conflict pathway. It will extend Gross's process model of emotion regulation by specifying a novel mechanism for dysregulation leading to outward-directed problems. Findings could inform targeted interventions that address maladaptive emotion goals to reduce aggression.
proposals.novelty: While past work connects suppression to poor internalizing outcomes, testing its role in externalizing problems via heightened expressive tension and negative affect is novel. Integrating norms, goals, and precise psychophysiological measures (corrugator EMG) to unpack this specific pathway moves beyond correlational self-report studies dominant in the field pre-2026.

proposals.title: Interacting Stages: A Test of the Non-Independence of Attentional Deployment and Cognitive Change in Emotion Regulation
proposals.motivation: The process model of emotion regulation (Gross, 1998, 2015) posits distinct, sequential stages (e.g., situation selection, attentional deployment, cognitive change, response modulation). Research typically tests strategies in isolation, implicitly assuming stage independence. However, real-world regulation is dynamic; strategies may interact, with the efficacy of a later-stage strategy (e.g., cognitive reappraisal) potentially being enhanced or diminished by an earlier-stage action (e.g., attentional distraction). Testing this assumption is crucial for moving from a static model to a dynamic, ecologically valid understanding of regulatory success and failure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the regulatory strategies of attentional deployment and cognitive change interact non-additively. Specifically, we predict that brief prior use of attentional distraction will potentiate the subsequent effectiveness of cognitive reappraisal in reducing negative emotional experience and corrugator supercilii (frown muscle) activity, compared to using either strategy alone or in the reverse order.
proposals.method: We will use a within-subjects, multi-method design. Participants will view standardized negative emotional images (IAPS) under four counterbalanced conditions: distraction-only, reappraisal-only, distraction-then-reappraisal, and a watch-neutral baseline. Emotion goals will be instructed for each block. We will measure experiential responses via continuous self-report, expressive behavior via corrugator EMG, and peripheral physiology (e.g., skin conductance). The key analysis will involve 2 (Distraction: yes/no) x 2 (Reappraisal: yes/no) repeated-measures ANOVAs on emotion outcome measures, with a focus on the significant interaction term to test non-independence.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct experimental test of the stage independence assumption in Gross's process model. It will advance theory by clarifying whether emotion regulation stages function as isolated levers or as an interactive system. Practically, it could inform the design of more effective, multi-step intervention protocols (e.g., for clinical samples as studied with the ERQ-S) that sequence strategies for maximal impact.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines individual strategies or compares their efficacy, no pre-2026 study has explicitly manipulated the sequential combination of process model stages to test for statistical interactions on multi-modal emotional outcomes, moving beyond simple additive comparisons.

proposals.title: Cultural Variation in the Process Model of Emotion Regulation: A Cross-Cultural Test of Situation Selection and Attentional Deployment
proposals.motivation: James Gross's process model of emotion regulation is a foundational framework in affective science, but its development and validation have been primarily within Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic (WEIRD) populations. Pre-2026 research in cultural psychology consistently shows that emotion generation and valuation are culturally shaped, raising a critical question: Are the proposed regulatory processes (e.g., situation selection, attentional deployment) universally applicable and utilized in the same way? This gap limits the model's generalizability and its utility for designing interventions in diverse cultural contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative preference for and effectiveness of early-stage regulation strategies (situation selection, attentional deployment) will vary systematically across cultures, influenced by culturally normative emotion goals (e.g., influence of interdependence vs. independence). Specifically, individuals from more interdependent cultures will show a greater preference for situation selection over attentional deployment when regulating negative social emotions compared to those from independent cultures.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method, cross-cultural design comparing participants from the United States (independent culture) and Japan (interdependent culture). Using Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) as both assessment and a light-touch intervention platform, participants will report on daily emotion-eliciting situations, their chosen regulation strategies, and emotion goals. In a controlled lab session, we will use a standardized emotion induction (e.g., viewing negative social scenes) and measure experiential, expressive (via corrugator supercilii EMG), and physiological responses while participants are instructed to use either situation selection (choosing to avoid or approach a subsequent stimulus) or attentional deployment. The design will test both habitual use (EMA) and causal efficacy (lab) of these processes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct experimental test of cultural neutrality in the process model's core mechanisms. It will yield novel data on how cultural context shapes the trajectory of the emotion regulation process from goal formation to strategy selection. The findings will critically advance affective science by moving the field from a universalist assumption toward a culturally-informed model of regulation, with direct implications for tailoring clinical tools like the ERQ for global use.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has compared emotion expression or experience across cultures, this proposal uniquely investigates cultural variation in the *process* of regulation itself, integrating Gross's process model with cultural psychology frameworks on emotion goals. The combination of EMA tracking of naturalistic strategy use with precise psychophysiological measurement (corrugator) in a cross-cultural experimental design is a novel approach to this question.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of the Process Model of Emotion Regulation Questionnaire-Observer Report (PMERQ-OR)
proposals.motivation: Self-report measures of emotion regulation (ER), like the PMERQ, are limited by self-presentation biases and introspective ability. An observer-report version would provide a crucial multi-method perspective, essential for clinical assessment (e.g., for disorders where self-insight is impaired) and for studying interpersonal emotion regulation dynamics. This aligns with the field's push for ecologically valid, multi-informant assessment, as seen in Gross's recent work using ecological momentary assessment and psychophysiological measures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the PMERQ-OR will demonstrate strong convergent validity with self-reported PMERQ scores and discriminant validity from general personality traits. Furthermore, we predict that observer-reported ER strategy use will correlate more strongly with objective behavioral and physiological indices of regulation (e.g., corrugator supercilii EMG during emotion induction) than self-report alone.
proposals.method: We will adapt the PMERQ items into third-person format (e.g., 'He/she typically...'). In Study 1, a large community sample of dyads (e.g., close friends, romantic partners) will complete the PMERQ-OR about their partner and the PMERQ about themselves. We will conduct exploratory and confirmatory factor analyses to establish structure and internal consistency. In Study 2, a subset of participants will undergo a lab-based mixed-emotion induction task (e.g., using film clips), during which we measure experiential (self-report), expressive (facial EMG, specifically corrugator activity), and physiological (e.g., heart rate) responses. Observers will view recordings of the targets and complete the PMERQ-OR.
proposals.contribution: This project will yield the first validated observer-report measure of ER strategies based on the process model, directly extending Gross's questionnaire work. It will provide a new tool for clinicians and researchers to obtain more objective assessments of ER. The findings will also advance understanding of the correspondence between self- and other-perceptions of regulation and their links to objective outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While observer reports exist for traits, creating one specifically for the dynamic, strategy-specific PMERQ framework is novel. The integration of observer report with multi-modal lab-based measures (experience, expression, physiology) to validate a questionnaire extends beyond typical psychometric validation, mirroring the multi-method approach in Gross's recent experimental work on mixed emotions.

proposals.title: Profiles of Maladaptive Emotion Regulation: A Person-Centered Analysis Linking PMERQ Strategy Use to Psychopathology and Physiology
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on emotion regulation (ER) has largely focused on adaptive strategies (e.g., reappraisal) or examined maladaptive strategies (e.g., suppression, rumination) in isolation. However, clinical reality suggests individuals use complex, co-occurring patterns of maladaptive strategies. The Process Model of Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (PMERQ), which assesses strategy use across the emotion-generative timeline, provides a framework to identify these real-world profiles and their distinct links to psychopathology, a critical gap in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: Latent Profile Analysis (LPA) of PMERQ subscales will reveal distinct, replicable profiles of maladaptive ER strategy use (e.g., a pervasive dysregulation profile, a preemptive avoidance profile). These profiles will be differentially associated with specific internalizing and externalizing symptoms and will manifest in unique physiological signatures (e.g., corrugator EMG reactivity, heart rate variability) during a mixed-emotion induction task.
proposals.method: We will recruit a large, diverse sample (N=500) oversampled for clinical symptoms. Participants will complete the PMERQ and diagnostic interviews. Using LPA in Mplus, we will model profiles based on PMERQ maladaptive strategy subscales (e.g., avoidance, rumination, suppression). Profile stability will be tested via split-sample validation. In a lab subsample (n=150), we will employ Gross's mixed-emotion film paradigm, measuring experiential reports, expressive behavior (via automated facial action coding of corrugator supercilii and zygomaticus major), and physiological responses (EMG, ECG) to test profile-based differences in emotional reactivity and regulation.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field from a variable-centered to a person-centered understanding of ER dysregulation, providing an empirically derived taxonomy of clinically relevant ER profiles. It will establish concrete physiological and expressive correlates of these profiles, offering multi-method validation and potential biomarkers for targeted intervention. The findings will directly inform the development of more personalized, mechanism-focused treatments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has linked single strategies to outcomes, this is the first to use the PMERQ's process-oriented framework to identify naturalistic *combinations* of maladaptive strategies as person-centered profiles and to validate them against a multi-system physiological criterion, moving beyond self-report.

proposals.title: Beyond Valence: The Differential Effects of Arousal-Focused and Action-Tendency-Focused Emotion Goals on Regulation Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on emotion regulation, including Gross's own work, predominantly conceptualizes emotion goals in terms of valence (e.g., increase positive, decrease negative). However, core affect theory and functionalist perspectives posit that arousal and action tendencies are fundamental, independent dimensions of emotional experience. This narrow focus on valence limits our understanding of the full spectrum of regulatory motives and their consequences for well-being and social functioning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (1) individuals will spontaneously adopt and can be instructed to adopt emotion goals targeting specific arousal levels (e.g., calm down) or action tendencies (e.g., reduce urge to approach), and (2) these non-valence goals will produce distinct patterns of experiential, expressive (e.g., corrugator EMG), and physiological outcomes compared to valence-focused goals.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method, within-subjects experimental design. Participants will be exposed to standardized affective film clips designed to elicit specific emotions (e.g., anger, fear, excitement). Using Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) prompts delivered via smartphone, participants will be randomly assigned, post-induction, to implement one of three goal types: valence-focused (e.g., feel less negative), arousal-focused (e.g., feel calmer), or action-tendency-focused (e.g., feel less impulsive). Experiential reports, facial electromyography (corrugator supercilii and zygomaticus major), and electrodermal activity (arousal index) will be continuously monitored. Data will be analyzed using multilevel modeling to compare the regulatory trajectories across goal conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic evidence that emotion regulation is guided by a broader set of goals than valence alone, directly testing a core but neglected assumption of functionalist emotion theories. It will expand the theoretical framework of the process model of emotion regulation and offer more nuanced targets for clinical interventions (e.g., targeting high arousal in anxiety rather than just negative affect).
proposals.novelty: While past work has examined goals for mixed emotions or positivity/negativity, explicitly manipulating and measuring the outcomes of goals targeting arousal and action tendencies as primary regulatory endpoints is a novel and critical extension of the emotion regulation literature.

proposals.title: Implicit Priming of Emotion Regulation Goals: A Multi-Method Comparison with Explicit Instruction
proposals.motivation: James J. Gross's foundational work has established that explicit emotion regulation goals powerfully shape emotional experience, expression, and physiology. However, in daily life, regulation often occurs without conscious deliberation. It is unknown whether implicit cues or contextual primes can automatically engage regulatory processes, offering a more ecologically valid model for spontaneous regulation and potential low-burden intervention pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implicit contextual primes (e.g., subliminal words, goal-relevant environmental cues) will induce emotion regulation effects comparable to those produced by explicit instruction, as measured by self-reported experience, expressive behavior (corrugator supercilii EMG), and physiological arousal.
proposals.method: We will adapt Gross's established explicit instruction paradigm for regulating negative emotions (e.g., increase, decrease, accept). In a within-subjects design, participants will view standardized negative affective images under three conditions: 1) Explicit goal instruction, 2) Implicit priming via masked lexical stimuli (e.g., 'calm', 'control') presented before each image, and 3) a no-goal baseline. Dependent measures will include continuous self-report, corrugator EMG activity, and skin conductance response. Data will be analyzed using multilevel modeling to compare the magnitude and time course of regulation effects across conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test the necessity of conscious intent for emotion regulation, bridging laboratory studies of explicit goals with real-world, implicit regulatory processes. It will contribute a novel experimental paradigm for studying automatic regulation and inform the design of ecological momentary interventions that use subtle environmental cues to support mental health.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined implicit emotion regulation as a trait or individual difference, this proposal innovatively tests the causal efficacy of experimentally manipulated implicit primes as a direct analog to explicit instructions, a comparison not yet made in Gross's pre-2026 research program.

proposals.title: Regulating Fleeting Feelings: The Role of Emotion Goals in Shaping Responses to Subliminal and Micro-Expression Stimuli
proposals.motivation: Prior research on emotion regulation, including Gross's work on mixed emotions, has largely relied on conscious, sustained emotional stimuli. However, real-world emotional challenges often involve rapid, pre-conscious cues, such as fleeting facial expressions. Understanding how individuals regulate emotional responses to these brief, subliminal signals is crucial for developing a complete model of adaptive emotion regulation in dynamic social contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-instructed emotion goals (e.g., to feel positive, to feel negative, to feel balanced) will differentially modulate experiential, expressive (corrugator supercilii EMG), and physiological responses to subliminal and micro-expression stimuli, even when conscious awareness of the stimulus is absent.
proposals.method: We will adapt a mixed-emotion paradigm using brief (≤500ms) and subliminal (≤17ms, backward-masked) emotional images and facial micro-expressions. Participants will be randomly assigned to different emotion goal conditions before each block. Dependent measures will include self-reported affect, continuous corrugator supercilii EMG activity (a validated index of negative affect), and skin conductance response. Stimulus presentation and timing will be controlled using PsychoPy, and EMG data will be processed using established pre-2026 methods (e.g., high-pass filtering, rectification, smoothing) to capture micro-variations in expressive activity.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend the Process Model of Emotion Regulation by testing its applicability to the earliest, pre-conscious stages of the emotion generative process. It will provide novel evidence on whether regulatory goals can shape responses before full conscious appraisal, offering insights into the automaticity of regulation. Findings could inform interventions for social anxiety or interpersonal sensitivity where reactions to subtle cues are maladaptive.
proposals.novelty: While Gross's lab has studied mixed emotion regulation and used corrugator EMG, applying this framework to subliminal and micro-expression stimuli to probe pre-conscious regulatory mechanisms is a novel integration. It moves beyond conscious, sustained stimulus presentation to test regulation in an ecologically valid, temporally fine-grained context not yet explored in this line of work.

proposals.title: Regulating the Group: How Individual Emotion Regulation of Mixed States Influences Emotional Coactivation and Reciprocity in Dyads and Triads
proposals.motivation: Emotional contagion is a well-documented group-level phenomenon, but pre-2026 research has largely examined it as a passive, automatic process. The role of deliberate emotion regulation (ER), a core theme of James J. Gross's work, in shaping this contagion is poorly understood. This is critical because in real-world groups (e.g., families, teams), individuals often regulate their own mixed emotional states, and the downstream effects on collective emotional dynamics remain unmeasured.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an individual's use of cognitive reappraisal to regulate their own mixed emotions will reduce negative emotional coactivation (corrugator supercilii EMG synchrony) and emotional reciprocity (lagged response coupling) within a group, compared to expressive suppression or a no-regulation control.
proposals.method: We will adapt Gross's established mixed-emotion film paradigm and ER goal manipulation for a group context. Participants in dyads/triads will view films while one member is randomly assigned an ER goal (reappraisal, suppression, watch). We will measure experiential (EMA via smartphone), expressive (facial EMG: corrugator and zygomaticus), and physiological (heart rate, skin conductance) responses continuously. To quantify group-level coactivation, we will compute windowed cross-correlation and multilevel vector autoregression (mlVAR) models on the time-series data, testing for synchrony and directional influence.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend Gross's process model of emotion regulation from the individual to the interpersonal sphere, providing the first experimental evidence for how specific regulation strategies propagate through a group's emotional response systems. It will offer a novel methodological framework for analyzing multi-person, multi-channel affective data to model emotional contagion as a dynamic regulatory process.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied individual regulation and passive contagion separately, this proposal uniquely treats the regulator as an active node within a group's emotional network, testing how their regulated output systematically alters the network's connectivity, moving beyond descriptive correlation to causal modeling of interpersonal influence.

proposals.title: Inferring Emotion Regulation Goals from Multimodal Physiological and Expressive Signatures: A Machine Learning Approach
proposals.motivation: Emotion regulation research has established that goals (e.g., to increase or decrease emotion) powerfully shape regulatory outcomes. However, current methods rely on self-report to identify these goals, which is intrusive and susceptible to bias. A model that can infer regulation goals from objective, real-time psychophysiology would enable unobtrusive assessment and personalized, just-in-time adaptive interventions in affective computing and clinical science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patterns of facial electromyography (corrugator supercilii), electrodermal activity, and cardiovascular activity, combined with automated facial expression analysis, contain sufficient information to reliably classify whether an individual is pursuing a goal to increase or decrease their emotional response to a stimulus.
proposals.method: Participants will be instructed to adopt either an increase or decrease emotion goal while viewing standardized affective images. We will collect continuous physiological data (corrugator EMG, EDA, ECG) and video for automated facial action unit (AU) extraction via OpenFace. Using pre-2026 machine learning, we will train and compare a set of classifiers (e.g., Support Vector Machines, Random Forests, and a simple LSTM recurrent neural network) on feature vectors of physiological and expressive data to predict the instructed regulation goal.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated computational model for inferring emotion regulation goals from peripheral physiology and expression. It will contribute a novel methodological tool for ecological momentary assessment and extend Gross's process model by empirically mapping the link between the goal and response modulation stages. The resulting algorithms could form the basis for next-generation emotion-aware systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Gross and others has shown that goals *lead to* physiological changes, the inverse problem—using physiology to *infer* the latent goal—remains unaddressed. This proposal flips the causal direction of standard analysis, applying pattern classification to a core question in emotion regulation.

proposals.title: Aberrant Positive-Negative Emotion Coactivation and Reciprocity in Clinical Dysregulation: A 3D-ESM Investigation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on emotion dysregulation in clinical disorders (e.g., depression, borderline personality disorder) has focused primarily on deficits in regulating negative affect. However, the 'dual problem' of dysregulation—encompassing both negative and positive emotion systems—is poorly understood in daily life. The 3D-ESM framework (differentiation, dysregulation, distance) provides a novel lens to examine if the typical patterns of coactivation and reciprocity between positive and negative emotions are disrupted in clinical populations, moving beyond static lab assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that clinical populations characterized by emotion dysregulation will exhibit aberrant temporal dynamics between positive and negative emotions in daily life, specifically showing reduced negative reciprocity (where positive affect fails to downregulate subsequent negative affect) and increased negative coactivation (simultaneous experience of high positive and high negative affect) compared to healthy controls.
proposals.method: We will employ a 3D-ESM protocol over 14 days with participants diagnosed with Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) and matched controls. Using smartphones, participants will complete 6 random prompts daily assessing current positive and negative emotion states. We will apply pre-2026 multilevel vector autoregressive (mlVAR) modeling to the time-series data to quantify within-person emotion networks, specifically estimating lagged reciprocity and contemporaneous coactivation parameters. Corrugator supercilii EMG will be collected in a subset of lab visits to validate self-reported negative affect and explore physiological-expressive concordance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first ecological, dynamic systems account of the dual positive-negative emotion dysregulation problem in MDD. It will extend Gross's process model of emotion regulation by identifying which specific temporal dynamics (reciprocity, coactivation) are disrupted in a real-world context. Findings could inform the development of more nuanced, dynamic ESM-based interventions targeting maladaptive emotion patterns.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used ESM to study affect dynamics, applying the 3D-ESM framework to explicitly test theories of coactivation and reciprocity in a clinical sample is novel. Integrating mlVAR network analysis with a physiological measure (corrugator) in an ESM design moves beyond self-report to capture multi-system dysregulation.

proposals.title: Parsing the Pathways: A Test of Emotion Regulation's Unique Mediating Role in Well-Being Against Stress and Social Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Emotion regulation (ER) is widely posited as a central mechanism linking psychological traits to well-being. However, pre-2026 research often treats ER as a monolithic mediator without rigorously testing if its effects are redundant with other established pathways, such as reduced physiological stress reactivity or increased positive social interaction. Determining the unique explanatory power of ER is critical for refining theoretical models and targeting interventions more precisely.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the statistical mediation effect of emotion regulation on the relationship between dispositional factors (e.g., neuroticism) and well-being outcomes will be significantly attenuated or rendered non-significant when simultaneously modeled alongside stress reactivity (cortisol, heart rate variability) and objective measures of social engagement.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 community participants to complete a 7-day ecological momentary assessment (EMA) protocol. Using a smartphone app, participants will report emotion goals, regulation attempts, and social context 5 times daily. Concurrently, they will wear an Empatica E4 wristband to continuously measure physiological stress (electrodermal activity, heart rate) and corrugator supercilii (frown muscle) activity via integrated electromyography. We will use multilevel structural equation modeling (MSEM) to conduct parallel mediation analyses, pitting self-reported ER (from EMA and the ERQ-S) against aggregated physiological stress indices and GPS/audio-derived social interaction metrics as simultaneous mediators.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrative, multi-method test of emotion regulation's unique mediational role. It will clarify whether ER is a primary, independent mechanism or a correlate of more fundamental stress and social processes, thereby advancing a more nuanced theory of emotional health. The findings will directly inform whether interventions should target ER skills specifically or broader lifestyle factors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined ER as a mediator, this proposal's novelty lies in its direct, pre-registered competition between ER and alternative mechanistic pathways using high-frequency, multi-channel data (experiential, expressive, physiological, social) to adjudicate their unique contributions.

proposals.title: Psychophysiological Mechanisms of Emotion Regulation During Nature Exposure: The Role of Heart Rate Variability and Corrugator EMG
proposals.motivation: Research prior to 2026 indicates that exposure to natural environments can reduce stress and improve mood, but the underlying psychophysiological mechanisms of how nature facilitates emotion regulation (ER) remain poorly understood. Objective, multi-modal measurement is critical to move beyond self-report and understand the dynamics of regulatory capacity. This study directly addresses a gap in James J. Gross's work on the physiological correlates of emotion regulation by applying it to a potent, ecologically valid context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that brief nature exposure, compared to an urban control setting, will enhance emotion regulation capacity, as evidenced by increased high-frequency heart rate variability (HF-HRV) at rest and a more adaptive (e.g., faster recovery) psychophysiological response profile (HRV and corrugator supercilii EMG) during a subsequent laboratory-based emotion regulation task.
proposals.method: Using a within-subjects design, participants will complete a 20-minute exposure (nature walk vs. urban walk) followed by an emotion regulation task involving negative image viewing with instructions to either reappraise or suppress. We will collect continuous ECG to derive HF-HRV (analyzed via frequency-domain methods) and corrugator EMG. Primary analyses will use mixed-effects models to test the interaction between exposure condition, regulation strategy (goal), and time on physiological trajectories and recovery.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, psychophysiological account of how natural environments confer emotional benefits by objectively quantifying their impact on regulatory capacity. It will extend Gross's process model of emotion regulation into real-world contexts and offer empirical evidence for nature-based interventions. The findings could inform the design of targeted supports for individuals with ER difficulties.
proposals.novelty: While nature exposure and emotion regulation have been studied separately, this proposal novelly integrates them by using established lab-based psychophysiology (HRV, EMG) to measure how a real-world antecedent (nature) alters subsequent regulatory function, moving beyond simple mood assessment to a capacity-based model.

proposals.title: Cultural Variation in Emotion Socialization Goals and Practices: A Multi-Method Test of the Universality of the Emotion Coaching Model
proposals.motivation: The dominant emotion coaching model, which emphasizes validating and discussing children's emotions, is rooted in Western, individualistic cultural contexts. Pre-2026 research suggests cultural differences in emotion valuation and regulation, yet the model is often presented as universally beneficial. This study directly tests its cultural assumptions, which is critical for developing equitable and effective interventions in diverse societies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (1) parents' emotion socialization goals (e.g., valuing calmness vs. emotional expression) will vary systematically across cultural groups, and (2) the association between emotion coaching practices and child emotional well-being will be moderated by cultural context and parental emotion goals.
proposals.method: We will recruit parent-child dyads from three cultural groups (e.g., European American, East Asian, Latinx) in the U.S. (N=300). Using Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA), parents will report daily emotion socialization practices and child affect. In a lab session, dyads will discuss an emotional event while we record expressive (facial EMG, e.g., corrugator supercilii) and experiential responses. Parents will complete the ERQ-S and a novel measure of culturally-informed emotion goals. Analyses will use multilevel modeling to test within- and between-culture effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated, multi-method test of cultural variation in core tenets of the emotion coaching framework. It will yield data to adapt the model for diverse populations or propose culturally-situated alternatives, advancing the science of emotion socialization beyond a one-size-fits-all approach.
proposals.novelty: While cultural differences in emotion are known, this study uniquely combines Gross lab methods (EMA, psychophysiology, ERQ) with a direct, critical test of a specific, influential intervention model's universality, moving from documenting differences to challenging applied frameworks.

proposals.title: Sustained Effects of an Ecological Momentary Assessment Intervention on Parent-Child Emotion Dynamics: A Two-Year Longitudinal Efficacy Trial
proposals.motivation: Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) shows promise as a real-time intervention for improving parent-child emotion conversations, but its long-term efficacy is unknown. Pre-2026 research indicates that early emotion socialization is critical for child development, yet most interventions lack evidence of sustained impact. A longitudinal trial is needed to determine if EMA-driven changes in parental behavior and child emotion regulation persist beyond the intervention period.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that parents who complete a 4-week emotion-focused EMA intervention will demonstrate sustained increases in supportive emotion socialization behaviors and report greater child emotion regulation skills, compared to a waitlist control group, at 6-, 12-, and 24-month follow-ups.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm randomized controlled trial with 200 parent-child dyads (children aged 4-6). The intervention group will complete a 4-week EMA protocol, receiving prompts 3x daily to report on their own and their child's emotions and engage in guided conversation. The control group will receive standard psychoeducation materials. Primary outcomes include parent-reported child emotion regulation (ERC), observed parent emotion coaching behaviors from lab visits, and child expressive physiology (corrugator supercilii EMG reactivity during emotion-eliciting tasks). Multi-level modeling will assess growth trajectories and intervention effects across 6-, 12-, and 24-month assessments.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous, long-term test of an EMA-based micro-intervention for family emotion processes. It will clarify whether brief, real-time supports can create lasting shifts in parent-child interaction patterns and child emotional development. Findings will inform the scalability and design of low-burden, technology-enhanced preventive interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Gross and others has used EMA to measure or prompt emotion regulation in adults, this study uniquely applies it as a longitudinal intervention tool targeting dyadic parent-child processes and tracks sustained developmental outcomes over years, moving beyond acute lab-based or short-term effects.

proposals.title: A Micro-Intervention Approach to Just-in-Time Emotion Coaching: An Ecological Momentary Assessment and Intervention Study
proposals.motivation: Effective emotion regulation is critical for well-being, yet traditional interventions are often poorly timed and burdensome. Drawing on the success of micro-interventions in mHealth for behaviors like smoking cessation, this project proposes that delivering bite-sized, context-sensitive emotion coaching prompts can enhance the efficacy and ecological validity of emotion regulation support. This addresses a key gap in translating Gross's process model of emotion regulation into scalable, real-world tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that context-triggered micro-interventions, delivered via smartphone, will lead to greater increases in the use of adaptive emotion regulation strategies (e.g., cognitive reappraisal) and more positive emotional outcomes (e.g., reduced negative affect, increased positive affect) compared to a control condition receiving generic, non-contextual prompts.
proposals.method: We will use a 14-day Ecological Momentary Assessment and Intervention (EMAI) design with 120 adult participants. A smartphone app will deliver 3-5 micro-intervention prompts daily, triggered by passive sensor data (GPS, time) and self-reported negative affect from brief EMA surveys. Prompts will be based on Gross's process model (e.g., "Try to think about this situation from a different perspective"). We will measure experiential outcomes via EMA, expressive outcomes via front-facing camera recordings of corrugator supercilii activity, and self-reported strategy use via the ERQ-S. A randomized controlled trial will compare this active EMAI group to an assessment-only control.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel framework for structuring just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) in emotion science, moving beyond assessment to active, theory-driven coaching. It will yield empirical evidence on the efficacy of micro-delivery of emotion regulation support and generate fine-grained data on the temporal dynamics of strategy use and emotional response in daily life.
proposals.novelty: While EMA is established for assessment, applying mHealth micro-intervention design principles to structure real-time, context-sensitive emotion *coaching* prompts represents a significant methodological innovation. This integrates health behavior change techniques directly into the study of emotion regulation processes.

proposals.title: A Tailored Ecological Momentary Intervention for Emotion Regulation in Parents of Children with Emotional and Behavioral Disorders: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Parents of children with emotional or behavioral disorders (EBD) experience chronic, high-stress caregiving demands that deplete their regulatory resources, increasing risk for parental psychopathology and impairing the quality of parent-child emotion conversations critical for child development. Pre-2026 work by Gross and others shows that brief, in-the-moment supports can improve emotion regulation, but existing interventions are not sufficiently intensive or tailored for this clinically distressed population's unique needs and fluctuating emotion goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tailored Ecological Momentary Intervention (EMI), which delivers context-sensitive prompts for emotion regulation strategies based on real-time assessment of parental stress and emotion goals, will lead to greater improvements in parental well-being (reduced negative affect, physiological arousal) and observed conversational quality compared to a standard psychoeducation control.
proposals.method: We will adapt the EMA-as-intervention framework from Gross's prior work for high-stress clinical parents. Using a smartphone app, 120 parents of children with EBD will be randomized to the 4-week EMI or an active control. The EMI algorithm will use twice-daily EMA surveys (stress, emotion goals) and continuous corrugator supercilii EMG via a wearable sensor to trigger personalized intervention prompts. These prompts will recommend specific regulation strategies (e.g., cognitive reappraisal, situation modification) based on a decision-tree algorithm that integrates the participant's baseline ERQ-S scores, current stress level, and pre-identified emotion goal (e.g., decrease negativity vs. increase positivity).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a scalable, mechanistically-informed intervention model for supporting parental emotion regulation in high-stress contexts. It will advance the field by demonstrating how real-time biosensor data (corrugator EMG) can be integrated with self-report to personalize regulatory support, and by testing the efficacy of a tailored EMI in a clinical population where it is most needed.
proposals.novelty: While EMAs have been used as interventions, the novelty lies in intensifying and tailoring the approach for a clinical parent population by algorithmically integrating continuous physiological data (corrugator EMG) with dynamic emotion goal assessments to drive personalized strategy suggestions in real time.

proposals.title: Disentangling Cognitive Effort from Affective Change: A Multi-Method Investigation of Corrugator Supercilii Activity During Reappraisal
proposals.motivation: Corrugator supercilii activity is a widely used, pre-2026 psychophysiological index of negative affect, central to studies of emotion regulation (ER). However, its specificity is unclear, as cognitive reappraisal—a core ER strategy—inherently involves cognitive effort, which may also influence facial muscle tension. If corrugator reduction during reappraisal primarily reflects a decrease in cognitive effort rather than negative affect, it challenges the validity of a foundational measure in affective science.
