proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reductions in corrugator electromyographic (EMG) activity during the cognitive reappraisal of negative stimuli are more strongly associated with concurrent reductions in self-reported and physiological indices of cognitive effort (e.g., pupil dilation, heart rate variability) than with reductions in self-reported negative affect or other physiological affect indices (e.g., skin conductance).
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 participants to complete a standard reappraisal task using high-arousal negative images from the IAPS. In a within-subjects design, participants will view images under three conditions: passive viewing, reappraisal to decrease negative affect, and a matched cognitive effort control (e.g., a complex visual analysis task). We will measure continuous corrugator EMG, skin conductance, pupil dilation (via eye-tracking), and heart rate variability. Self-reported emotion and perceived effort will be collected after each trial. Data will be analyzed using multilevel modeling to partition the variance in corrugator change attributable to self-reported affect versus effort indices.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide critical evidence for or against the specificity of corrugator EMG as a pure index of negative affect during regulation. It will advance methodological rigor in psychophysiology by delineating the confound of cognitive effort. The findings will directly inform the interpretation of decades of ER research and guide future measurement selection.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted the effortful nature of reappraisal, no pre-2026 study has directly pitted the cognitive effort reduction account against the affective change account for corrugator activity using a comprehensive multi-method design with a matched effort control condition.

proposals.title: Priming Positive Affect to Facilitate Negative Affect Reduction: A Test of Sequential Emotion Regulation Efficacy
proposals.motivation: Standard emotion regulation (ER) interventions, such as cognitive reappraisal, often target negative affect (NA) directly. However, pre-2026 work in Gross's lab and others suggests that positive affect (PA) and NA are not simple opposites and that PA can build psychological resources. This proposal tests a novel, sequential approach to regulation, examining whether strategically increasing PA first can create a more receptive state for subsequent NA reduction, potentially offering a more efficient intervention model for clinical and subclinical populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequential regulation strategy (first increasing PA, then reducing NA) will lead to a greater and faster reduction in self-reported and physiologically-indexed NA compared to a strategy focused solely on reducing NA from the outset.
proposals.method: We will conduct a within-subjects lab experiment with three conditions: (1) PA-then-NA (sequential), (2) NA-only (standard reappraisal), and (3) a watch-neutral control. Participants will view standardized negative image sets (IAPS). In the sequential condition, they will first be instructed to use a positive reappraisal goal (e.g., find a silver lining) to increase PA, followed by a standard negative reappraisal goal to reduce NA. Dependent measures will include continuous self-reported valence/arousal, facial EMG (corrugator supercilii and zygomaticus major activity), and heart rate variability. Data will be analyzed using multilevel modeling to compare the trajectory and final level of NA reduction across conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct experimental test of a sequential, PA-priming model of emotion regulation. It will contribute to basic science by clarifying the temporal dynamics and potential synergies between different regulation goals. Clinically, it could inform the development of more nuanced and potent intervention sequences for disorders characterized by high negative affect and low positive affect, such as depression.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined mixed emotions and the role of emotion goals, the deliberate, goal-directed sequencing of a positive affect increase as a precursor to enhance negative affect reduction is a novel and non-obvious inversion of the standard regulatory sequence.

proposals.title: Sequential Emotion Regulation in Autobiographical Contexts: Testing the Temporal Dynamics of Prolonged Negative Memory Regulation
proposals.motivation: Laboratory studies of emotion regulation (ER) often use brief, standardized stimuli, limiting ecological validity. It is unknown whether the sequential patterns of regulation (e.g., early expressive changes followed by later subjective shifts) observed in lab settings with short films or images generalize to the regulation of prolonged, personally-relevant negative memories. Testing this is crucial for translating ER science into real-world interventions for conditions like depression and PTSD, where regulation targets are complex autobiographical events.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during the deliberate regulation of prolonged autobiographical negative memories, a sequential pattern will emerge where physiological and expressive responses (e.g., corrugator supercilii EMG) change earlier in the regulation process than self-reported subjective experience, mirroring patterns found with brief laboratory stimuli.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 participants to complete a multi-session study. In session one, participants will identify a high-intensity negative autobiographical memory. In session two, using Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) prompts on smartphones, they will be cued to recall this memory for a prolonged period (3-5 minutes) under different instructed regulation goals (e.g., reappraise, suppress, accept). Continuous physiological (corrugator EMG, skin conductance, heart rate) and facial expression (via webcam for automated FACS coding) data will be collected. Subjective emotion ratings will be obtained at multiple timepoints post-recall. Time-series analyses will compare the latency and magnitude of change across experiential, expressive, and physiological channels.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct test of the temporal dynamics of ER during prolonged, real-world emotional events. It will clarify whether foundational lab-based models of ER sequence hold under more ecologically valid conditions. The findings will inform the design of just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) that target specific response systems at optimal timepoints during real-world emotion regulation episodes.
proposals.novelty: While Gross's lab has studied sequential patterns and mixed emotions with brief stimuli, applying this precise temporal framework to extended, autobiographical recall is novel. Combining prolonged recall, multi-channel assessment (including corrugator EMG), and EMA-based ecological triggering moves beyond static lab paradigms to capture dynamic regulation in a personally-significant context.

proposals.title: Temporal Dynamics of Negative Affect Generation: An Ecological Momentary Assessment and Psychophysiological Study
proposals.motivation: While James J. Gross's process model of emotion regulation and extensive research have detailed how emotions are regulated *down* from an elicited state, the antecedent processes of how neutral states escalate into clinically relevant negative affect are less understood. This gap limits our ability to design early, preventative interventions for mood disorders, which often involve the problematic generation of negative emotion. A detailed temporal analysis of generation, mirroring methods used in regulation studies, is crucial for a complete model of emotional functioning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the generation of negative affect from a neutral state follows a distinct, quantifiable temporal signature in experience, expression (e.g., corrugator supercilii EMG), and physiology, and that individual differences in baseline emotion goals will moderate the steepness and peak of this generative trajectory.
proposals.method: We will use Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) with integrated psychophysiology. Participants will be prompted 8 times daily for 7 days to report current affect and emotion goals (via ERQ-S). During lab-based sessions, participants will view a series of neutral images followed by negative image probes while continuous physiological data (corrugator EMG, EDA, ECG) is recorded. We will apply multilevel growth curve modeling and vector autoregressive (VAR) time-series analysis to the EMA data to model real-world generation dynamics, and use similar temporal decomposition techniques (e.g., extracting early, mid, and late response components) on the lab-based psychophysiological data to map the micro-genesis of negative affect.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, multi-method temporal map of negative affect generation in daily life and the lab, directly complementing existing models of emotion regulation. It will establish whether emotion generation has a predictable dynamic structure and identify modifiable factors (e.g., antecedent goals) that influence this trajectory, offering novel targets for clinical intervention aimed at preventing negative emotional spirals.
proposals.novelty: This proposal innovatively applies the rigorous temporal analysis frameworks typically reserved for studying emotion *regulation* to the understudied process of emotion *generation*, creating a direct methodological inverse. It uniquely integrates lab-based psychophysiology (corrugator) with EMA to bridge moment-to-moment and day-to-day timescales of affective generation.

proposals.title: Temporal Dynamics of Emotion Regulation in Clinical Populations: A Multi-Modal Investigation of Negative and Positive Affect Change During Reappraisal
proposals.motivation: James J. Gross's pre-2026 work has established reappraisal as a core emotion regulation strategy, but its efficacy and dynamics in clinical populations with regulation deficits remain poorly understood. Specifically, it is unknown whether disorders like Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) and Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) alter the fundamental temporal sequence—the latency and order—of negative affect (NA) reduction and positive affect (PA) generation during regulation, which could inform more targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals with MDD or GAD will exhibit a delayed reduction in NA and a blunted or absent increase in PA during cognitive reappraisal compared to healthy controls, reflecting a dysregulated temporal unfolding of the regulatory process.
proposals.method: We will recruit three age- and gender-matched groups: MDD, GAD, and healthy controls (n=45 each). Participants will complete a well-validated reappraisal task (e.g., viewing negative IAPS images under 'reappraise' vs. 'look' instructions). We will use Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) to capture high-density, real-time self-reports of NA and PA (0-100 scales) at multiple timepoints post-stimulus. Concurrently, we will measure expressive physiology (corrugator supercilii EMG for NA, zygomaticus major EMG for PA) and heart rate variability. Latency and order of change will be analyzed using multilevel growth curve modeling and cross-correlation of NA and PA time-series data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, multi-modal characterization of the temporal dynamics of reappraisal in clinical populations. It will clarify whether regulation deficits stem from a failure to initiate NA reduction, a failure to generate PA, or a decoupling of these processes. The findings will directly inform the development of timing-sensitive clinical tools, such as just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) delivered via EMA platforms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has compared mean regulation success across groups, this proposal innovatively focuses on the *micro-temporal dynamics* (latency/order) of NA and PA change, a mechanistic question unexplored in clinical samples. Integrating Gross's EMA-as-intervention framework with his multi-method (experience, expression, physiology) approach to study temporal dynamics in pathology is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: The Intensity Cascade: How Momentary Negative Emotion Intensity Predicts Subsequent Regulation Strategy Choice in Daily Life
proposals.motivation: While emotion regulation (ER) is a dynamic, within-person process, most research treats intensity and regulation choice as static or concurrent. Understanding the temporal sequence—whether the intensity of a negative emotion predicts *which* regulation strategy is deployed moments later—is crucial for developing adaptive, just-in-time interventions. This addresses a core gap in Gross's process model by testing its temporal assumptions in real-world contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher momentary intensity of a negative emotion will predict a greater subsequent likelihood of selecting expressive suppression over cognitive reappraisal within individuals, as high intensity may overwhelm cognitive resources required for reappraisal.
proposals.method: We will use ecological momentary assessment (EMA) with signal- and event-contingent sampling over 14 days. Participants will report negative emotion intensity and their primary regulation strategy for the most significant event since the last prompt. We will employ multilevel vector autoregressive (mlVAR) modeling and lagged multilevel logistic regression to analyze within-person, temporal dependencies, controlling for emotion type, context, and individual differences in trait ER (ERQ-S).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical test of a temporal 'intensity cascade' in naturalistic ER, moving beyond cross-sectional correlations. It will clarify a potential mechanism for regulation failure and inform the design of dynamic, personalized interventions that consider emotional intensity as a precursor to regulation choice.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely applies intensive longitudinal, lagged analyses to test a directional, within-person hypothesis about intensity driving regulation selection, a temporal claim implied but not directly tested in Gross's process model or prior EMA work on emotion goals.

proposals.title: A Just-in-Time Adaptive Intervention (JITAI) for Regulating High-Intensity Negative Emotion: Integrating Ecological Momentary Assessment with Real-Time Feedback
proposals.motivation: Effective emotion regulation is critical for mental health, but existing interventions often lack ecological validity and fail to provide support in the moment of need. Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) has shown promise for monitoring emotions, yet its use as a direct, real-time intervention component remains underdeveloped. This project aims to bridge this gap by creating a JITAI that delivers personalized regulation strategies precisely when self-reported emotional intensity is high and regulation attempts are reported as failing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a JITAI delivering tailored emotion regulation strategy prompts in response to real-time EMA reports of high negative emotion intensity and low perceived regulation success will lead to greater reductions in subsequent negative emotion intensity and corrugator supercilii (frown muscle) activity, compared to a control condition receiving non-contingent, generic prompts.
proposals.method: Participants will complete a 7-day EMA protocol, reporting emotion intensity and regulation success 8 times daily. Using a micro-randomized trial design, on a subset of prompts, participants will be randomized to receive either a JITAI prompt or a control message. The JITAI algorithm will be triggered only when both self-reported negative emotion intensity and perceived regulation failure exceed person-mean thresholds. The intervention will use a reinforcement learning framework (e.g., a contextual bandit algorithm) to select from a suite of strategy prompts (e.g., cognitive reappraisal, mindfulness) based on individual and contextual factors (e.g., location, social context). Ambulatory corrugator EMG will be collected to index expressive physiology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for a closed-loop, data-driven intervention that moves beyond assessment to active, context-sensitive support. It will yield novel insights into the temporal dynamics of regulation success and failure in daily life. Furthermore, it will advance methodology by integrating real-time self-report with physiological sensing and adaptive algorithmic decision-making for mental health support.
proposals.novelty: While EMA-as-intervention and JITAIs exist, this proposal uniquely focuses on the *interaction* of high emotion intensity and *self-identified regulation failure* as the precise trigger for support, and it tests the efficacy of this approach using a multi-modal combination of experiential, expressive (corrugator EMG), and algorithmic data.

proposals.title: The Intensity Threshold: Causal Effects of Emotion Intensity on the Selection and Efficacy of Regulation Strategies
proposals.motivation: While emotion regulation (ER) choice is central to adaptive functioning, the causal role of emotional intensity in driving these choices remains poorly understood. Prior correlational and self-report work suggests intensity matters, but experimental manipulation is needed to establish causality and clarify whether high intensity prompts reliance on automatic strategies (like suppression) or motivates effortful engagement (like reappraisal).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that experimentally induced high emotional intensity will cause a shift in regulation choice towards more automatic, expressive-modulation strategies (e.g., suppression) over cognitively demanding strategies (e.g., reappraisal), and that this shift will be associated with distinct patterns of experiential, expressive (corrugator EMG), and physiological outcomes.
proposals.method: We will use a within-subjects design with two factors: Emotion Induction Intensity (Low vs. High, manipulated via standardized film clips with validated intensity ratings) and Regulation Instruction (Watch, Reappraise, Suppress). Participants will complete trials in all conditions while we measure self-reported affect, facial EMG (corrugator supercilii), and heart rate. The primary analysis will use multilevel modeling to test the causal effect of the Intensity factor on the choice of a preferred strategy in a subsequent free-choice block, and on the multi-modal outcomes during instructed regulation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first experimental evidence for a causal intensity threshold in ER choice, moving beyond correlational findings. It will clarify the real-time trade-offs between regulatory effort and effectiveness under high arousal, directly informing models of ER as a dynamic, context-sensitive process.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely combines an experimental manipulation of emotional intensity with a multi-method assessment (including corrugator EMG) to test causal mechanisms in ER choice, a significant advance over prior survey-based or single-intensity induction studies.

proposals.title: Developing and Validating a Computerized Adaptive Test for the Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (ERQ-CAT): Precision and Efficiency in Clinical and Ecological Assessment
proposals.motivation: The Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (ERQ) is a foundational self-report measure of cognitive reappraisal and expressive suppression, widely used in both research and clinical practice. However, its fixed-length format can be burdensome in intensive longitudinal designs like Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA), which are central to modern emotion regulation research, and may lack precision for individual-level clinical assessment. Computerized Adaptive Testing (CAT), which tailors item selection to the individual's estimated trait level, offers a solution by reducing participant burden while maintaining or improving measurement precision, but has not been systematically applied to emotion regulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a CAT version of the ERQ (ERQ-CAT) will achieve measurement precision equivalent to the full ERQ with significantly fewer items, and that this efficiency gain will not compromise its convergent validity with established behavioral (e.g., corrugator EMG during emotion regulation tasks) and experiential measures of emotion regulation.
proposals.method: We will develop the ERQ-CAT using a two-phase approach. First, we will calibrate the ERQ item bank using a large, pre-existing dataset (N > 2000) and apply Item Response Theory (IRT), specifically the Graded Response Model, to estimate item parameters (discrimination, difficulty). Second, in a new validation study, participants will complete the ERQ-CAT, the full ERQ, and a laboratory-based emotion regulation task where goal-directed regulation of negative and positive stimuli is measured via self-report, expressive behavior (corrugator EMG), and physiology. The CAT algorithm will use a maximum information item selection rule and a Bayesian expected a posteriori (EAP) trait estimation method, terminating when the standard error of measurement falls below a pre-set threshold.
proposals.contribution: This project will yield a validated, efficient adaptive measure of emotion regulation strategy use, directly enabling more precise and less burdensome assessment in clinical settings and high-frequency ecological studies. It will provide a methodological bridge between Gross's foundational work on emotion regulation and the growing need for dynamic, personalized assessment tools.
proposals.novelty: While CAT is established in other fields (e.g., psychopathology), its application to core emotion regulation constructs is novel. This project innovatively integrates IRT-based adaptive testing with multi-method validation (experiential, expressive, physiological) characteristic of Gross's lab, moving beyond pure self-report validation.

proposals.title: Concurrent Validation of the Emotion Regulation Questionnaire-Short Form (ERQ-S): Linking Self-Reported Strategy Use to Physiological Reactivity During Emotion Induction
proposals.motivation: The Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (ERQ) and its short form (ERQ-S) are foundational self-report tools for assessing cognitive reappraisal and expressive suppression. However, their validation has primarily relied on other self-report measures, creating a mono-method bias. Establishing robust, multi-method evidence of validity by linking ERQ-S scores to objective physiological indices of emotion regulation success is a critical next step for the field's measurement science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher self-reported habitual use of reappraisal (ERQ-S Reappraisal subscale) will be associated with more adaptive physiological profiles (e.g., faster heart rate variability recovery) during negative emotion induction, while higher self-reported habitual suppression (ERQ-S Suppression subscale) will be associated with less adaptive profiles (e.g., heightened and sustained corrugator supercilii EMG activity).
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 participants who will complete the ERQ-S. In the lab, they will undergo a standardized negative emotion induction (e.g., viewing high-arousal negative IAPS images). We will continuously record electrocardiography (ECG) to derive high-frequency heart rate variability (HF-HRV) as an index of parasympathetic flexibility, and electromyography (EMG) from the corrugator supercilii muscle as an index of negative affective expression. Using multilevel modeling, we will analyze the trajectory of physiological recovery post-induction, with ERQ-S subscale scores as between-subjects predictors of these recovery slopes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, concurrent validation of the ERQ-S against well-established physiological markers of emotion regulation. It will strengthen the instrument's utility in clinical and experimental settings by grounding self-report in biological processes. The findings will also clarify the distinct physiological correlates of reappraisal and suppression as measured by the ERQ-S.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Gross and others has linked emotion regulation strategies to physiology, no study has yet used a standardized emotion induction to systematically test the ERQ-S as a predictor of dynamic physiological recovery patterns, offering a novel and rigorous test of its concurrent validity.

proposals.title: Implicit Social Learning of Emotion Regulation: Testing the Sufficiency of Observational Contagion
proposals.motivation: Emotion regulation (ER) training is often resource-intensive, requiring explicit instruction. If observing regulated emotional expressions can spontaneously improve an observer's own regulatory capacity, it would reveal a powerful, low-cost pathway for adaptive emotional influence. This directly addresses core questions in Gross's research on the social functions of emotion regulation and its dissemination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that observing a social partner's regulated emotional expressions (e.g., using cognitive reappraisal) in response to negative stimuli, without any explicit training or instruction, will be sufficient to induce emotional contagion of the regulated state, leading to reduced negative experience and expressive physiology in the observer.
proposals.method: Dyads of participants will complete a task where one member (the 'Regulator') views negative images and is instructed to use reappraisal. The other member (the 'Observer') views the same images but is given no regulation instructions; they only see a live video feed of the Regulator's face. Using facial electromyography (EMG), we will measure the Observer's corrugator supercilii activity as a physiological index of negative emotional contagion. Self-reported emotion and eye-tracking data will be collected to assess attention and experience. The key comparison is between Observer responses when viewing a Regulator using reappraisal versus when viewing a Regulator responding naturally.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct test of whether regulated expressions alone can serve as an implicit social learning mechanism for ER, extending models of emotional contagion. It will identify the necessary conditions for the passive transfer of regulatory skill, with implications for designing scalable interventions in families, schools, and clinical settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work shows emotional states are contagious, and that explicit training works, it is non-obvious whether the specific behavioral signature of a regulated process (not just a positive state) is sufficient for contagion without any declarative knowledge or goal-setting by the observer.

proposals.title: Chronic Unpredictable Stress and Emotion Regulation: A Longitudinal Study of Brain-Heart Coupling Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Chronic unpredictable life stress (CUS) is a major risk factor for affective disorders, yet its impact on the dynamic, real-time interplay between neural and autonomic systems during emotion regulation remains poorly understood. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Gross's work on emotion goals and physiological responses, has largely relied on lab-based, cross-sectional designs, limiting ecological validity and temporal resolution for understanding adaptation to prolonged threat.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals exposed to CUS will exhibit a dysregulated pattern of brain-heart coupling—specifically, a weakened association between prefrontal cortical activity (indexing regulatory effort) and heart rate variability (indexing parasympathetic flexibility)—during attempts to regulate negative emotion, and that this dysregulation will predict subsequent emotional dysfunction over time.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 participants (60 high-CUS, 60 low-CUS) using the Daily Inventory of Stressful Events. Over a 14-day ecological momentary assessment (EMA) period, participants will complete brief emotion regulation tasks (e.g., reappraisal, acceptance) 3x daily via a smartphone app, which triggers concurrent, ambulatory electroencephalography (EEG) and electrocardiography (ECG) using a lightweight, wearable system (e.g., a modified Muse headband and Polar H10 sensor). Brain-heart coupling will be quantified using time-frequency analysis (EEG alpha/beta power) and heart rate variability (HRV; RMSSD, HF power), with their dynamic correlation computed using windowed cross-correlation algorithms. Self-reported emotion, emotion goals, and corrugator EMG will be collected via EMA.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, ecologically valid map of how chronic stress alters the psychophysiological architecture of emotion regulation in daily life. It will extend the ERQ framework by identifying a potential biomarker (brain-heart dyscoupling) for regulatory vulnerability. The findings could inform the development of just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) for stress-related psychopathology.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond single-session lab studies to capture the temporal dynamics of brain-heart coupling under real-world, sustained threat, integrating Dr. Gross's expertise in emotion goals, EMA-as-intervention, and physiological measurement (e.g., corrugator) into a novel, ambulatory multi-system framework.

proposals.title: Personalizing Treatment for Emotional Dysregulation: A Non-Linear Computational Model of Synergistic Modality Sequencing
proposals.motivation: Current treatments for emotional dysregulation (e.g., CBT, mindfulness) are often delivered as static, one-size-fits-all protocols, despite evidence that individual differences in baseline factors like alexithymia and social-emotional goals moderate efficacy. Furthermore, the field lacks a principled framework for determining the optimal sequence or combination of these modalities, which may yield non-linear, synergistic effects critical for durable change. This gap limits the personalization and potential effectiveness of interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the synergistic efficacy of sequenced emotion regulation interventions (e.g., cognitive reappraisal training followed by mindfulness) is non-linearly contingent on the interactive profile of a patient's pre-treatment characteristics, including alexithymia (ERQ-S), emotion goal orientation, and baseline expressive physiology (corrugator EMG).
proposals.method: We will use an experimental therapeutics framework with multiple baseline assessments and a longitudinal, within-subjects design where participants receive two core interventions (cognitive reappraisal, mindfulness) in counterbalanced sequences. Multi-modal data (self-report via EMA/ERQ-S, expressive physiology via corrugator EMG, and experiential sampling) will be collected pre-, during, and post-intervention. We will employ machine learning techniques, specifically Random Forests and Gradient Boosting Machines (pre-2026 standards), to model the non-linear interactions between baseline profiles and treatment sequence outcomes, identifying predictive combinations of features for optimal sequencing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical test of a non-linear, person-specific model for sequencing emotion regulation interventions. It will yield a preliminary computational tool for predicting optimal treatment pathways and generate novel hypotheses about the mechanisms of synergistic change, moving the field beyond static protocol comparisons toward dynamic, personalized intervention science.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond comparing static treatments to modeling how their *sequence* creates synergy, and uniquely posits that this synergy is governed by non-linear interactions within a person's pre-treatment psychological architecture, a hypothesis not yet tested in emotion regulation research.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Dynamics of Emotion Regulation and Pain Modulation: A Multi-Modal fMRI and Ecological Momentary Assessment Study
proposals.motivation: Chronic pain and emotional distress are highly comorbid, suggesting shared regulatory systems. Pre-2026 cross-sectional neuroimaging studies (e.g., involving the anterior cingulate and prefrontal cortices) indicate overlap in the neural circuitry for pain modulation and emotion regulation, but the temporal dynamics and causal interactions between these systems remain poorly understood. A longitudinal design is critical to determine whether improvements in emotion regulation capacity drive neuroplastic changes in pain modulatory circuits, or vice versa, informing more targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal improvements in the habitual use of cognitive reappraisal (an emotion regulation strategy) will predict increased functional connectivity within central pain modulatory networks (e.g., descending pain inhibitory pathways) over time, and that these neural changes will mediate reductions in daily pain sensitivity and negative affect.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 participants with subclinical affective and pain symptoms for a 12-month study with four assessment waves. At each wave, participants will undergo fMRI during a well-validated pain-induction task (e.g., thermal stimuli) and an emotion regulation task (e.g., reappraisal of negative images). Seed-based functional connectivity analyses will focus on key regions like the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex and periaqueductal gray. Between scans, participants will complete Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) protocols 3x daily for 2-week bursts, reporting pain, affect, and emotion regulation goal use, with corrugator supercilii EMG collected via wearable sensors to index expressive regulation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal map of co-developing emotion and pain regulation systems, identifying neural predictors of resilience. It will extend Gross's work on emotion regulation goals and EMA interventions by linking real-world regulatory efforts to long-term neurofunctional change. Findings will offer mechanistic insights for developing combined psychological and neuromodulatory treatments for pain-affective disorders.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined these systems in isolation or cross-sectionally, this proposal uniquely integrates repeated multi-modal fMRI with intensive longitudinal behavioral and physiological (corrugator EMG) data to model temporal precedence and bidirectional influences between emotion and pain regulation at neural and experiential levels.

proposals.title: Divergent Emotion Regulation Pathways from Social Norms: A Multimodal Predictive Modeling Study of Internalizing and Externalizing Symptom Development
proposals.motivation: Shared etiological factors, such as rigid social-emotional norms, can lead to divergent psychopathological outcomes (e.g., depression vs. aggression), but the specific emotional and cognitive mechanisms driving this split are poorly understood. Pre-2026 research by Gross and others highlights that emotion regulation goals and strategies are central to mental health, yet predictive models linking shared risk to distinct symptom pathways via multimodal mechanism data are lacking. This gap limits our ability to design targeted, mechanism-based interventions for at-risk individuals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct patterns of emotional reactivity and regulation—specifically, heightened threat sensitivity and expressive suppression leading to internalizing symptoms, versus reward hyposensitivity and expressive disinhibition leading to externalizing symptoms—will mediate the divergence from a shared etiological baseline of punitive social-emotional norms, and that these patterns can be reliably identified using multimodal data.
proposals.method: We will recruit a longitudinal adolescent cohort at risk due to exposure to rigid social-emotional norms. Using Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA), we will collect self-reported emotion goals and experience, alongside psychophysical measures (corrugator EMG, heart rate). Laboratory sessions will add behavioral tasks (e.g., emotional conflict, reward processing) and structural/functional neuroimaging (amygdala, ventral striatum, prefrontal cortex connectivity). We will use a multi-stage machine learning pipeline: first, unsupervised learning (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Models) to identify latent symptom pathway clusters; then, supervised models (e.g., Regularized Logistic Regression, Random Forests) to predict pathway membership from baseline multimodal features; finally, mediation analyses to validate the proposed emotional and cognitive mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a predictive, mechanism-based model of psychopathological divergence, moving beyond correlational studies. It will directly inform the development of targeted interventions (e.g., cognitive reappraisal training for the internalizing pathway, emotion modulation training for the externalizing pathway) by identifying pre-symptomatic markers. It will also extend Gross's work on emotion regulation by embedding it within a developmental, multimodal predictive framework.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using multimodal data not merely for outcome prediction, but to empirically validate the divergent mechanistic pathways that explain *how* a shared etiology branches, integrating laboratory physiology (e.g., corrugator) with real-world EMA and neuroimaging to capture the emotion regulation process in vivo.

proposals.title: Beyond Conscious Strategy: Measuring Automatic Interpersonal Emotion Regulation in Daily Life
proposals.motivation: James J. Gross's foundational work on emotion regulation has largely focused on conscious, strategic processes (e.g., reappraisal, suppression). However, much of social interaction and interpersonal emotion regulation (IER) likely occurs automatically and habitually, outside deliberate awareness. Understanding these automatic IER behaviors is critical for developing a complete model of how emotions are regulated in real-world social contexts, with implications for relationship quality and mental health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals will exhibit reliable, measurable patterns of automatic interpersonal emotion regulation (e.g., facial mimicry, vocal tone modulation, proximity changes) in response to a partner's emotional expressions, and that the frequency and type of these automatic behaviors will be predicted by relationship quality and individual differences in trait emotion regulation (ERQ-S scores), independent of self-reported conscious regulation goals.
proposals.method: We will use Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) with sensor-fusion to capture automatic IER in parent-child dyads. Parents and children will wear lightweight, research-grade sensors (e.g., Affectiva's Q-Sensor 2.0 for electrodermal activity, a miniature corrugator EMG sensor for frowning) and audio recorders during structured home interactions. A novel algorithm will analyze the multi-modal data stream to detect temporal contingencies between the child's vocal/physiological arousal (the trigger) and the parent's subsequent, rapid (≤500ms) physiological (corrugator, EDA) and paralinguistic vocal responses (pitch, speech rate), indexing automatic regulation attempts. These sensor-derived 'automatic IER events' will be compared to EMA self-reports of conscious regulation goals.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first multi-method, sensor-based framework for quantifying automatic interpersonal emotion regulation in naturalistic settings. It will extend Gross's process model of emotion regulation by empirically validating a non-conscious, habitual pathway for IER. The findings will inform the design of more nuanced ecological interventions, like those in Gross's prior EMA work, that target both conscious and automatic regulatory processes within relationships.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied conscious IER goals or isolated automatic mimicry, this proposal innovatively integrates multi-modal sensing with EMA to capture and distinguish automatic IER *behaviors* from strategic ones in real-time dyadic interaction, moving beyond the field's dominant assumption of conscious intentionality.

proposals.title: Neural Mechanisms of Social Support in Emotion Regulation: An fMRI Study of Supportive Message Processing
proposals.motivation: Social support is a critical factor in emotional well-being, yet the neural mechanisms through which different types of supportive messages facilitate emotion regulation remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 research by Gross and others has established the importance of emotion goals and regulatory strategies, but has not directly linked the real-time processing of supportive social input to specific brain networks involved in regulation. This gap limits the development of targeted, neuroscience-informed interventions for individuals struggling with emotion dysregulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that supportive messages high in cognitive reframing content will engage prefrontal cortical regions (e.g., dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, dlPFC) associated with cognitive reappraisal, whereas messages high in emotional validation will engage socio-affective networks (e.g., ventromedial PFC, temporoparietal junction), and that the degree of activity in these networks will predict successful down-regulation of negative affect as measured by self-report and corrugator supercilii EMG.
proposals.method: We will use functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) with a block design. Participants will first undergo an autobiographical recall task to induce a negative emotional state, confirmed via self-report and corrugator EMG. They will then view pre-validated supportive messages varying in content (cognitive reframing vs. emotional validation vs. neutral control) while undergoing scanning. Whole-brain analysis will be conducted using SPM12 or FSL, with general linear models (GLM) to identify blood-oxygen-level-dependent (BOLD) responses associated with message type. Psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses will test functional connectivity between seed regions (e.g., amygdala) and regulatory networks.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct neural evidence linking specific features of supportive social communication to the brain's emotion regulation circuitry. It will extend Gross's model of emotion regulation by identifying the neural substrates of socially-aided regulation. The findings will inform the design of more effective, neurologically-grounded supportive interventions, such as those used in ecological momentary assessment (EMA) platforms, by pinpointing the active ingredients of helpful messages.
proposals.novelty: While prior fMRI work has examined reappraisal in isolation, this study uniquely investigates the brain's processing of *externally provided* regulatory scaffolds (supportive messages). It integrates social support theory with affective neuroscience by treating supportive messages as naturalistic stimuli that engage distinct neural pathways depending on their regulatory target (cognition vs. affect).

proposals.title: Linguistic Markers of Interpersonal Emotion Regulation: A Conversational Analysis of Dyadic Interactions
proposals.motivation: Interpersonal emotion regulation (IER) is a core social process, but its real-time, in-situ dynamics are poorly understood. Pre-2026 research, including Gross's work on parent-child conversations and emotion goals, has relied heavily on self-report and isolated physiological measures (e.g., corrugator EMG), missing the moment-to-moment discursive exchange where regulation is enacted. A fine-grained analysis of natural conversation is needed to identify the specific linguistic mechanisms through which one person attempts to alter another's emotional state.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, quantifiable linguistic features (e.g., pronoun use, question types, sentiment trajectories, and speech act sequences) within dyadic conversations will reliably distinguish between successful and unsuccessful interpersonal emotion regulation attempts.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and newly collected video/audio recordings of dyadic conversations (e.g., from conflict or support discussions). Using established Natural Language Processing (NLP) tools available pre-2026 (e.g., LIWC for psycholinguistic categories, Stanford CoreNLP for dependency parsing), we will extract fine-grained linguistic features from transcribed speech. We will employ sequential analysis and time-series modeling to examine how features in Speaker A's utterances predict changes in Speaker B's subsequent linguistic markers of emotion (e.g., emotional word frequency) and, where available, synchronized physiological data (e.g., corrugator supercilii activity).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first taxonomy of conversational markers of IER attempts, moving beyond broad constructs to specific, observable behaviors. It will bridge Gross's research on intrapersonal regulation goals and physiological responses with the interpersonal domain, offering an objective, language-based metric for studying regulatory success in dyads.
proposals.novelty: While discourse analysis is common in sociolinguistics, its systematic application to test hypotheses about the mechanisms of interpersonal emotion regulation—integrating NLP-derived linguistic features with physiological data—is a novel methodological advance for affective science pre-2026.

proposals.title: Micro-Moments of Support: The Impact of Brief Nonverbal Cues on Interpersonal Emotion Regulation and Physiological Co-Regulation
proposals.motivation: Interpersonal emotion regulation (IER) is crucial for well-being, yet most research examines broad strategies or conversations. The efficacy of brief, nonverbal micro-interventions in daily life remains poorly understood. Grounded in Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) and psychophysiological methods, this study addresses a critical gap by investigating how subtle, real-time supportive cues function as regulatory tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that receiving specific, goal-congruent nonverbal supportive cues (e.g., a gentle touch, a warm facial expression) from a partner will lead to immediate reductions in self-reported negative affect and corrugator supercilii (frown muscle) activity in the recipient, compared to neutral or incongruent cues.
proposals.method: Dyads (e.g., romantic partners) will participate in a controlled lab session where one member (the regulator) is trained to deliver specific nonverbal cues (touch, affirming nod, warm smile) in response to a signal, while the other (the target) undergoes a mild negative affect induction. We will use integrated psychophysiology (continuous corrugator EMG, heart rate) and experiential sampling (EMA via smartphone app prompting for emotion and goal ratings) to capture moment-by-moment responses. A factorial design will manipulate cue type and the target's pre-induction emotion goal (to enhance vs. reduce negative emotion) to test for goal-congruence effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fine-grained, multi-method evidence mapping specific nonverbal behaviors onto discrete psychophysiological and experiential regulatory outcomes. It will extend Gross's model of emotion regulation into the micro-temporal dynamics of IER and offer a mechanistic framework for designing targeted, just-in-time supportive interventions in naturalistic settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied nonverbal behavior or IER broadly, this proposal uniquely isolates and manipulates micro-moments of nonverbal support as discrete regulatory stimuli, testing their interaction with moment-to-moment emotion goals—a novel integration of Gross's emotion regulation framework with dyadic psychophysiology.

ideas.title: Dynamic Network Test of Reciprocal Influences Between Norms, Alexithymia, Emotion Regulation, and Symptoms
ideas.description: This study uses a multi-wave longitudinal panel design and Random-Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Modeling (RI-CLPM) to map the dynamic, reciprocal temporal relationships between social norms, alexithymia, emotion regulation, and psychological symptoms. This approach explicitly tests for bidirectional influences and feedback loops among all constructs, moving beyond the assumption of a simple unidirectional mediation chain. By separating within-person change from stable between-person differences, it provides a more rigorous and ecologically valid test of how these variables influence each other over time within individuals. The results will identify the most potent temporal pathways for potential intervention.

ideas.title: A Psychophysiological Network Model of Norms, Emotion Dysregulation, and Internalizing Symptoms
ideas.description: This research integrates network psychometrics with psychophysiology to construct and test a multi-level model of internalizing psychopathology. The network structure, which maps how specific social norms influence specific symptoms via emotional nodes (e.g., shame, anxiety), is not just statistically estimated but is grounded in concurrent biometric data like heart rate variability and electrodermal activity, which serve as objective indices of the proposed emotional dysregulation pathways. This allows researchers to test whether the strength of a hypothesized network connection between, for instance, a perfectionist norm and rumination is moderated by an individual's physiological capacity for emotion regulation. Consequently, the approach can distinguish between purely cognitive symptom associations and those underpinned by measurable physiological dysregulation, offering a more biologically plausible and clinically actionable model.

ideas.title: Non-linear Synergies in Multi-Modal Treatment Sequencing for Emotional Dysregulation
ideas.description: This research investigates the assumption that optimal treatment for emotional dysregulation is a single, static modality. It fuses this with the critique of linear sequential models by proposing an experimental and computational modeling approach. The core question is whether the synergistic effects of sequenced or combined therapeutic modalities are themselves contingent on non-linear, interactive relationships within the patient's pre-treatment psychological profile, such as the interplay between social norms, alexithymia, and baseline emotion regulation capacity. This would allow for the development of personalized, adaptive treatment sequences where the order and combination of interventions are dynamically optimized based on an individual's specific, interacting psychological features.

ideas.title: Modeling the Dynamic, Non-Linear Causal Architecture Between Norms, Alexithymia, and Emotion Regulation
ideas.description: This study uses a genetically informed, multi-wave longitudinal design to test for bidirectional and non-linear causal relationships between perceived social norms, alexithymia, and emotion regulation strategies. By collecting intensive repeated measures over two years and including sibling pairs (monozygotic/dizygotic twins or close-in-age siblings), the design can model latent change scores and control for shared familial confounds. This approach directly tests whether shifts in norms predict subsequent non-linear changes in alexithymia and regulation, and vice-versa, moving beyond cross-sectional correlation to establish temporal dynamics and strengthen causal inference.

ideas.title: Dynamic Network Modeling of Alexithymia and Emotion Regulation Using Ecological Momentary Assessment
ideas.description: This research uses Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) to intensively sample emotional experiences, perceived social norms, alexithymic difficulties, and regulation attempts in participants' natural environments over time. The dense, time-series data is then analyzed using dynamic network modeling and time-lagged regression techniques to map the non-linear and reciprocal relationships between these constructs. This approach reveals how momentary shifts in social context or internal state trigger specific regulatory pathways or alexithymic blocks, which in turn influence subsequent emotional events. Consequently, it uncovers the real-world, process-oriented architecture of emotional functioning, identifying key dynamic patterns that static, retrospective methods cannot capture.

ideas.title: Long-term efficacy trial of EMA intervention
ideas.description: Conduct a longitudinal study to measure the sustained impact of emotion-focused EMA on child emotional development and parent behavior.

ideas.title: Examine the inverse: externalizing symptoms pathway

ideas.title: Micro-interaction Pathways to Internalizing and Externalizing Symptoms
ideas.description: This research program investigates how conformity to specific emotional norms, observed in micro-interactions (e.g., a pause, a specific word choice) during conversations with partners or therapists, simultaneously creates risk for both internalizing and externalizing symptoms. By applying fine-grained sequential analysis to therapy or dyadic dialogue, it tests whether moments of norm enforcement predict immediate subsequent shifts in emotional mechanism—such as heightened shame (internalizing pathway) or anger suppression followed by outburst (externalizing pathway). This allows for a dynamic, process-oriented model that maps how singular social moments can seed divergent clinical outcomes over time.

ideas.title: Modeling Norms, Alexithymia, and Emotion Regulation as a Complex Adaptive System
ideas.description: This idea challenges the linearity of the sequential model by drawing an analogy from ecology, where complex, non-linear interactions between species and their environment are the norm. The analogous solution involves using causal pathway analysis (like SEM) and simulation modeling to uncover indirect and interactive effects. Adapted to psychology, the research would first use advanced statistical models (e.g., SEM with latent interaction terms) to test for moderated and mediated relationships between social norms, alexithymia, and emotion regulation. Subsequently, it would develop a theoretical computational model to simulate how these variables influence each other in a dynamic system with feedback, exploring scenarios where small changes in normative contexts lead to disproportionate, non-linear shifts in emotional processing outcomes.

ideas.title: Investigate the inverse: emotion dysregulation profiles

ideas.title: Disentangling Emotional Pathways: A Multi-Method Test of How Specific Norms Lead to Specific Externalizing Behaviors
ideas.description: This research precisely tests how a well-defined social norm (e.g., hypercompetitive peer norms) leads to distinct externalizing outcomes (e.g., reactive aggression, rule-breaking) through separable emotional mechanisms. Using a longitudinal design, it combines self-report, peer-report, and ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to measure in situ emotional states like frustration, anger, and callousness. Advanced modeling then isolates whether these emotional pathways are statistically distinct and differentially predictive of behavioral outcomes, moving beyond a vague 'different mechanisms' claim to a mechanistic model.

ideas.title: Multimodal Predictive Mapping of Divergent Symptom Pathways from Shared Etiological Norms
ideas.description: This research integrates multimodal data (behavioral, psychophysical, physical, neuroimaging) to build predictive models that map how shared etiological factors (e.g., specific social norms) diverge into distinct internalizing (e.g., depression) or externalizing (e.g., aggression) symptom pathways. The synergy lies in using the multimodal data not just for outcome prediction, but to identify and validate the divergent emotional and cognitive mechanisms (e.g., threat hypersensitivity vs. reward dysfunction) that explain the pathway split. The resulting models would enable mechanism-targeted interventions, predicting which individuals on a shared etiological risk trajectory are likely to develop externalizing versus internalizing problems and which treatment would best normalize their specific dysfunctional mechanism.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Neuroimaging of Pain-Emotion Dysregulation in the Development of Externalizing Pathways
ideas.description: This research program proposes a longitudinal study using repeated multimodal neuroimaging (e.g., fMRI during pain and emotion regulation tasks) and behavioral assessments to test a unified model. It investigates whether adverse social norms or environments lead to divergent developmental trajectories: one characterized by deficient pain modulation and internalizing symptoms, and another characterized by deficient emotion regulation (e.g., anger/irritability control) and externalizing symptoms like aggression. By tracking these systems concurrently from adolescence into young adulthood, the study can determine if an early imbalance or dyssynchrony in the maturation of these overlapping but distinct neural circuits predicts the specific behavioral pathway an individual follows, moving beyond correlation to identify potential mechanistic precursors.

ideas.title: Disentangling Reciprocal Causation: A Genetically-Informed Longitudinal Test of Internalizing Symptoms and Masculine Norm Conformity
ideas.description: This study uses a multi-method, longitudinal design to rigorously test for bidirectional causal pathways between internalizing symptoms, emotion regulation difficulties, and conformity to specific masculine norms. Data is collected across three time points from a sample of male-identified twins and siblings, incorporating self-report, behavioral tasks, and peer reports. Cross-lagged panel models analyze temporal precedence, while the discordant twin/sibling control model isolates the effect of within-family differences, controlling for shared genetic and environmental background factors. This approach clarifies whether symptoms drive norm conformity, conformity exacerbates symptoms, or a reciprocal, reinforcing cycle exists.

ideas.title: Use biometrics for concurrent validation
ideas.description: Validate ERQ-S scores against physiological measures like heart rate variability during emotion induction.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Emotion Regulation Questionnaire-Short Form (ERQ-S): Psychometric properties in a clinical sample.

ideas.title: Adapting Experience Sampling from Human-Computer Interaction to Assess Alexithymia
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Experience Sampling Method (ESM) as used in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) and Ubiquitous Computing, where in-situ probes are triggered by specific device interactions or contextual sensor data to assess user states like cognitive load. The analogous solution is adapted by using smartphone sensor data (e.g., location, activity, communication logs) to algorithmically detect contextually or socially salient moments in a participant's day. These moments then trigger Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) prompts designed to measure alexithymia and emotion regulation in real-time, moving beyond random sampling to capture emotional awareness challenges precisely within the daily contexts where they are most relevant and problematic.

ideas.title: Apply a Composite Stress Index from Materials Science to Emotion Regulation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in materials science, where composite indices integrate multiple stress measurements to predict system failure. The analogous solution is the use of criteria like the Tsai-Wu failure criterion, which combines tensile, compressive, and shear stress data into a single predictive metric for material fatigue. Adapted to psychophysiology, the research idea is to develop a multi-modal 'Emotion Regulation Load Index.' This would involve creating an algorithm to integrate real-time data streams from heart rate variability, electrodermal activity, facial electromyography, and respiratory sinus arrhythmia. The resulting composite biometric index would offer a more robust, objective, and predictive measure of the cumulative psychophysiological load on an individual's emotion regulation capacity, objectively assessing difficulty and signaling proximity to dysregulation.

ideas.title: Real-Time Emotional and Physiological Mapping of Sham Acupuncture's Placebo Components
ideas.description: This study integrates Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) with a sham electroacupuncture trial to investigate the pure placebo hypothesis. Participants undergoing sham or real electroacupuncture will complete EMA prompts multiple times per day to report their emotional awareness (alexithymia) and regulation in real-world contexts, while also wearing physiological monitors. By temporally linking high-frequency subjective emotional data with objective physiological measures, the research can model how moment-to-moment psychological states mediate or moderate any observed treatment effects, thereby testing if sham acupuncture's outcomes are fully explained by psychological placebo pathways.

ideas.title: Ecological Momentary Assessment of Prosocial Masculinity Dynamics and Internalizing Symptoms
ideas.description: This study uses smartphone-based ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to collect real-time, in-context data on the enactment of prosocial masculine norms, emotional states, and pain symptoms. By prompting participants multiple times per day, we can capture the immediate, within-person effects of behaviors aligned with positive masculinity (e.g., providing support, demonstrating resilience) on subsequent mood and pain fluctuations. This dynamic approach allows us to test if and when these norms act as a real-time buffer against internalizing symptoms and pain exacerbation, identifying specific situational triggers and protective pathways that are invisible in traditional survey research.

ideas.title: Micro-interaction analysis of therapeutic norm conformity to dissect distinct mind-body therapy mechanisms
ideas.description: This research program investigates whether CBT, MBSR, and acupuncture work through distinct mechanisms by analyzing the micro-interactions of therapeutic conversations. It shifts the focus from global constructs like 'expectation' to the detailed, moment-by-moment linguistic and paralinguistic markers of norm conformity (e.g., how a client aligns with a therapist's framing of an emotional experience). By comparing how each therapy's specific norms are enacted and reinforced in real-time dialogue about emotion, the study can identify unique, protocol-specific interactional pathways, moving beyond self-report to observe the distinct social machinery of each intervention.

ideas.title: Ecological Momentary Assessment of Culturally-Specific Pain and Mood Dynamics
ideas.description: This study uses smartphone-based ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to collect intensive longitudinal data on pain, mood, and context from men in specific non-Western cultural settings. By analyzing the real-time, within-person dynamics between pain flares and mood states, the research aims to identify and model culturally specific pathways (e.g., familial duty, stoicism, community integration) that actively shape the pain experience. This approach transcends simple replication to delineate how cultural context operates as a dynamic, proximal factor in daily life, rather than a static demographic variable. The resulting idiographic and nomothetic models will reveal actionable, culturally embedded intervention points for tailoring psychological treatments.

ideas.title: Micro-dosing nature contact for urban workers
ideas.description: Test the minimal effective 'dose' of brief, virtual or real nature exposure to improve emotion regulation at work.
ideas.sourceTitle: Associations of nature contact with emotional ill-being and well-being: the role of emotion regulation

ideas.title: Tracing Normative Stressors to Behavioral Eruption via Distinct Emotional Pathways
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Materials Science/Fracture Mechanics, where the same external stress leads to different material failures based on internal micro-pathways. The analogous problem of understanding how the same social norms lead to externalizing symptoms like aggression is solved by not treating 'emotion' as a monolithic mediator. Instead, the research would precisely trace how normative stressors are processed through distinct, high-resolution emotional mechanisms (e.g., simmering frustration vs. explosive shame-anger), analogous to tracking crack propagation. Using methods like ecological momentary assessment and dynamic systems modeling, the study would map these divergent emotional pathways to pinpoint specific intervention targets, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all model of norm-to-behavior linkage.

ideas.title: A Causal-Temporal Network Framework for Modeling Normative Influences on Symptom Dynamics
ideas.description: This research system combines intensive longitudinal methods (e.g., 30-day ecological momentary assessment) with large-scale multi-wave panel surveys to model how socio-cultural norms influence internalizing symptom networks over time. It applies time-series network analysis and causal discovery algorithms to the fine-grained, within-person data to identify robust, temporally-lagged pathways from perceived norms to emotional states to specific symptoms. The nomothetic panel data then tests the generalizability and stability of these identified dynamic pathways across a broader population, creating a robust model of normative influence on psychopathology that accounts for both causal direction and individual differences.

ideas.title: Micro-interaction Norm Conformity Analysis via Behavioral Signal Processing
ideas.description: This research adapts methods from computational neuroethology to study norm conformity in emotional conversations. The analogous problem—decoding the structure and function of animal social vocalizations—is solved by decomposing continuous vocal streams into discrete, measurable micro-units and analyzing their patterned production in specific social contexts. Adapted to this research, the idea is to scale down the analysis of norm conformity by applying a behavioral signal processing framework to human dialogue. Conversational turns about emotion would be recorded and processed to extract high-resolution micro-features: acoustic-prosodic features (fundamental frequency contours, speech rate, pause duration) and linguistic markers (specific word choices, filler words, affirmation phrases). The core analysis would examine how these micro-features in a speaker's utterance are modulated immediately following a partner's or therapist's prompt, probe, or emotional expression. This allows for the quantification of conformity to perceived interactional norms (e.g., reciprocity, validation, emotional granularity) at the sub-second, micro-sequential level within specific conversational moments, providing an objective, fine-grained measure of normative social coordination.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Resilience Theory to Model Masculine Norms as Stress Buffers
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that all masculine norms are uniformly harmful by investigating if some function as buffers against internalizing symptoms like depression and anxiety. Drawing an analogy from ecology and resilience science, where ecosystems are studied for structures (e.g., biodiversity) that confer adaptive capacity against disturbances, this adapted idea reframes the investigation. Instead of a deficit model, it posits certain masculine norms (e.g., emotional regulation, protective risk-taking) as potential psychosocial structures that may promote resilience. The study would empirically map these norms to specific emotional and cognitive pathways (e.g., reduced rumination, increased perceived control, proactive coping) to identify which, and under what contextual conditions, they buffer against internalizing psychopathology.

ideas.title: Challenge coactivation as the default mixed response

ideas.title: Multi-level analysis of gendered norms, policy mechanisms, and male emotional development
ideas.description: This research employs a multi-level, longitudinal design to investigate how specific national and sub-national policies and normative climates interact with individual and community factors to influence male emotional development. It collects repeated measures of alexithymia, internalizing symptoms, and related behaviors from a stratified cohort of males, nested within diverse regional units. These micro-level data are then linked to time-series data on discrete policy implementations and indicators of social norms, enabling analysis of cross-level effects and the identification of mediating mechanisms. The approach moves beyond ecological correlation to test how macro-level changes are filtered through local institutions and social networks to impact individual psychological outcomes.

ideas.title: Apply computational modeling to emotion system dynamics

ideas.title: Longitudinal Test of Psychosocial Mechanisms in Homogeneous Chronic Low Back Pain
ideas.description: This study investigates whether chronic low back pain, when rigorously defined as a homogeneous nociceptive condition, still exhibits heterogeneity in its longitudinal psychosocial pathways. Using a multi-wave longitudinal design, it tests if the sequential mediation model (from social/emotional norms to alexithymia to emotion regulation to pain and disability) holds uniformly for all patients within this homogeneous group. A null finding would support the core premise of Idea A—that the condition is fundamentally homogeneous in its key mechanisms—while significant variability in the strength or presence of this pathway would reveal critical, previously masked subgroups defined by psychosocial vulnerability rather than sensory phenotype.

ideas.title: Ecological Momentary Network Neuroscience for Pain
ideas.description: This research integrates smartphone-based Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) of pain and mood with repeated, short-burst neuroimaging sessions to create a dense, time-synchronized dataset of brain and behavior. By applying network neuroscience to the imaging data, we can model the brain as a dynamic system and identify how specific connectivity patterns shift in the minutes or hours surrounding real-world pain flares or relief. This allows us to move beyond static brain-pain correlations to model the causal-like, temporal dynamics of how therapy-induced network changes precipitate or follow changes in subjective experience. The ultimate goal is to identify the critical brain network states that are both predictive of real-time pain and most responsive to therapeutic intervention.

ideas.title: Use ecological momentary assessment for real-time data

ideas.sourceTitle: Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction, Cognitive Behavioral Therapy, and Acupuncture in Chronic Low Back Pain: Protocol for Two Linked Randomized Controlled Trials

ideas.title: Micro-Analysis of Normative Masculinity in Conversational Emotion Repair
ideas.description: This research investigates how specific masculine norms (e.g., violence, dominance, playboy) are behaviorally enacted and reinforced in micro-interactions during emotionally charged conversations with partners or therapists. By applying fine-grained discourse analysis (e.g., conversation analysis, linguistic inquiry) to recordings of these interactions, the study maps how momentary acts of norm conformity—such as joking to avoid vulnerability (playboy) or using declarative statements to shut down discussion (dominance)—directly impede emotion regulation and perpetuate alexithymia. This approach moves beyond correlational survey models to reveal the real-time, interactive 'how' of the pathway between masculine norm adherence and emotional dysfunction.

ideas.title: Disentangling Normative from Physiological Effects: Sham Acupuncture as a Tool to Isolate the Active Components of Masculine Norms on Emotional Pathways
ideas.description: This research program applies the core experimental logic of sham acupuncture studies—which seeks to separate the physiological effects of needling from the psychological effects of the treatment context—to the study of masculine norms. It proposes that adherence to certain masculine norms (e.g., exercise, stoic endurance, provider role) may confer mental health benefits through two distinct pathways: the psychological meaning and identity coherence they provide (analogous to a psychological placebo effect) and the tangible physiological or behavioral activities they prescribe (analogous to the active component of real acupuncture). By designing interventions that manipulate norm adherence with and without the associated behavioral/physiological components, this approach can uniquely test whether buffering effects against internalizing symptoms are mediated by belief, by action, or by their interaction.

ideas.title: Adapting Cognitive Neuroscience's Go/No-Go Task to Measure Implicit Norm Adherence
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from cognitive neuroscience—the Go/No-Go task, used to measure inhibitory control and prepotent response biases—to the problem of measuring implicit, non-conscious adherence to masculine norms. In the analogous field, the task reveals automatic cognitive processes through behavioral errors when participants must suppress a dominant response. The adapted research idea creates a behavioral paradigm where adherence to a masculine norm is the prepotent response. Participants are trained to respond quickly to stimuli congruent with a specific masculine norm. On critical 'No-Go' trials, stimuli that violate that norm are presented, and the participant must inhibit their response. The frequency of commission errors (i.e., automatically responding to a norm-violating concept) provides a direct, performance-based metric of the implicit strength of that normative association, offering a novel methodological complement to the latency-based measures of the Implicit Association Test.

ideas.title: Computational modeling of how prosocial masculine norms buffer pain-related learning and distress
ideas.description: This research direction integrates computational psychiatry models of pain with the study of positive masculinity. It uses reinforcement learning paradigms to quantify how adherence to prosocial masculine norms (e.g., courage, resilience for collective good) alters the subjective valuation of pain, the learning of pain-predictive cues, and decisions to engage in adaptive versus avoidant behaviors. This allows for testing mechanistic hypotheses about whether these norms buffer against internalizing symptoms by reducing threat sensitivity, increasing reward valuation of goal pursuit despite discomfort, or altering pain-related belief updating. The result is a formal, testable model of how a specific social identity factor computationally shapes the pain experience and emotional outcomes.

ideas.title: Context-Specific Co-Design of Interventions Targeting Maladaptive Expressions of Cultural Norms
ideas.description: This research begins by mapping the contextual functionality and harm of norms like emotional control and self-reliance within specific communities, identifying when they become maladaptive. Using participatory action research, interventions are co-designed with community members to target only these harmful expressions, framing changes as augmentations of existing values. The efficacy of these tailored interventions is then rigorously tested against both well-being metrics and community-defined measures of success, ensuring cultural validity and reducing backlash.

ideas.title: Decoupling Prosocial Competencies from Gender: A Test of the Predictive Utility of 'Masculinity' for Internalizing Symptom Buffering
ideas.description: This study investigates whether the mental health benefits attributed to 'positive masculinity' are better explained by underlying, non-gendered prosocial competencies. Using a mixed-methods, longitudinal design, the research first identifies a core set of emotional and communal competencies through factor analysis, independent of gendered framing. It then tests competing models to determine if framing these competencies as part of a masculine identity adds predictive value for buffering against depression and anxiety, or if the gendered construct introduces confounding effects from traditional norm adherence. The goal is to determine if promoting mental health requires reinforcing gender-linked models or can be more effectively achieved through universal, gender-neutral skill development.

ideas.title: Evaluating the Efficacy of CBT and MBSR for Pain in Men: The Moderating Role of Prosocial Masculinity
ideas.description: This study proposes a secondary analysis of existing CBT-for-pain and MBSR trial data, specifically examining outcomes for male participants. It will test the hypothesis that men who endorse prosocial masculine norms (e.g., courage in facing distress, resilience, protective responsibility) prior to treatment will demonstrate greater engagement, adherence, and clinically significant reductions in pain interference and internalizing symptoms compared to men who endorse traditional restrictive norms or wait-list controls. The research moves beyond simply comparing CBT and MBSR to investigate how culturally salient masculine identity frameworks can be leveraged to improve the real-world effectiveness of evidence-based psychological treatments for chronic pain in men.

ideas.title: Comparative efficacy of CBT versus MBSR for chronic pain in gender-diverse individuals assigned male at birth
ideas.description: This study proposes a randomized controlled trial to compare the efficacy of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for pain (pain-CBT) and Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) against a wait-list control specifically for transgender men and non-binary individuals assigned male at birth with chronic pain. It directly tests the assumption that intervention models developed and validated primarily in cisgender populations are equally applicable and effective for gender-diverse groups, who may face unique psychosocial stressors impacting pain. The analysis will examine not only relative effect sizes but also potential moderators like gender congruence and minority stress, thereby evaluating the interventions' cultural responsiveness. This synergy addresses a critical gap in both pain psychology and gender-affirming healthcare by generating evidence for tailored treatment recommendations.
